## Contextual Dynamic Pricing: Algorithms, Optimality, and Local Differential Privacy Constraints

Zifeng Zhao 1 , Feiyu Jiang 2 , Yi Yu 3 ∗

## Abstract

We study contextual dynamic pricing problems where a firm sells products to T sequentially-arriving consumers, behaving according to an unknown demand model. The firm aims to minimize its regret over a clairvoyant that knows the model in advance. The demand follows a generalized linear model (GLM), allowing for stochastic feature vectors in R d encoding product and consumer information. We first show the optimal regret is of order √ dT , up to logarithmic factors, improving existing upper bounds by a √ d factor. This optimal rate is materialized by two algorithms: a confidence bound-type algorithm and an explore-then-commit (ETC) algorithm. A key insight is an intrinsic connection between dynamic pricing and contextual multi-armed bandit problems with many arms with a careful discretization.

We further study contextual dynamic pricing under local differential privacy (LDP) constraints. We propose a stochastic gradient descent-based ETC algorithm achieving regret upper bounds of order d √ T//epsilon1 , up to logarithmic factors, where /epsilon1 &gt; 0 is the privacy parameter. The upper bounds with and without LDP constraints are matched by newly constructed minimax lower bounds, characterizing costs of privacy. Moreover, we extend our study to dynamic pricing under mixed privacy constraints, improving the privacy-utility tradeoff by leveraging public data. This is the first time such setting is studied in the dynamic pricing literature and our theoretical results seamlessly bridge dynamic pricing with and without LDP. Extensive numerical experiments and real data applications are conducted to illustrate the efficiency and practical value of our algorithms.

Keywords : Online learning, Confidence bound, Explore-then-commit, Differential privacy, Minimax optimality.

∗ 1. University of Notre Dame; 2. Fudan University; 3. University of Warwick

## 1 Introduction

With the technological advances and prevalence of online marketplaces, many firms can now dynamically make pricing decisions while having access to an abundance of contextual information such as consumer characteristics, product features and the economic environment. On the other hand, in practice, the demand model is unknown in advance and firms need to dynamically learn how the contextual information impacts consumer demand. Thus, to maximize its revenue, the firm needs to implement dynamic pricing, which aims to optimally balance the trade-off between learning the unknown demand function and earning revenues by exploiting the estimated demand model. Due to its importance in revenue management, dynamic pricing has been extensively studied across the fields of statistics, machine learning, and operations research under various settings. We refer the readers to Section S.1 for recent works and den Boer (2015) for a comprehensive review.

In this work, we consider the problem of contextual dynamic pricing, where the firm sells products to T ∈ N + sequentially arriving consumers and the unknown demand follows a generalized linear model (GLM). In particular, at each time period t ∈ { 1 , . . . , T } , the seller is given a d -dimensional feature vector z t ∈ R d , which encodes the contextual information, and needs to set a price p t in a compact price interval [ l, u ] . Given ( z t , p t ), the demand y t follows a GLM with an unknown parameter θ = ( α, β ) such that E ( y t | z t , p t ; θ ) = m ( α /latticetop z t -( β /latticetop z t ) p t ) , where m ( · ) is the inverse of the GLM link function. For example, we have m ( x ) = x for linear regression and m ( x ) = 1 / { 1+exp( -x ) } for logistic regression. Here, the α /latticetop z t term models the intrinsic utility of the product and β /latticetop z t captures the price elasticity. The revenue of the firm is defined as r t = p t y t . Due to its interpretability and flexibility, this GLM demand model including the linear special case (i.e. m ( x ) = x ) has been widely studied in the dynamic pricing literature under various scenarios (e.g. Ban and Keskin, 2021; Wang et al., 2024; Bastani et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2022c; Lei et al., 2023).

In the vanilla version of this problem, the firm aims to design a policy that utilizes the raw context { z t } and maximizes its expected revenue E ( ∑ T t =1 r t ) over the T periods (i.e. minimizes its regret over a clairvoyant that knows θ in advance). In this paper, we first propose two algorithms and

show that they achieve a near-optimal regret of order ˜ O ( √ dT ) under this setting, an improvement over the existing ˜ O ( d √ T ) regret upper bound in the literature (e.g. Ban and Keskin, 2021; Wang et al., 2024). In other words, the regret is sublinear in the dimension of the context vector.

To build intuition, consider the special (and simplified) case of a linear demand model with y t = α /latticetop z t -( β /latticetop z t ) p t + ε t , where { ε t } T t =1 is an i.i.d. mean zero error process. Denote e ( i ) as the i th standard basis of R d for i ∈ { 1 , . . . , d } . We further set the context vector z t = e ( /ceilingleft td/T /ceilingright ), assuming T is a multiplier of d . Under this simplified setting, we are essentially dealing with d independent dynamic pricing problems. In particular, the i th sub-problem runs for time t ∈ { ( i -1) T/d +1 , · · · , iT/d } with a total of T/d periods and assumes a linear demand model with no context (i.e. context-free). That is y t = α i -β i p t + ε t , where α i and β i denote the i th entry of α and β. The optimal regret of this contextfree sub-problem, due to Broder and Rusmevichientong (2012), is of order ˜ O ( √ T/d ). Summing up over all d sub-problems, intuitively, we can have a regret upper bound of order ˜ O ( d √ T/d ) = ˜ O ( √ dT ).

In recent years, there has been a growing concern over data privacy (Lucas et al., 2021), as consumers become increasingly aware of the data collected about them and their use in personalized pricing and recommendation. Based on differential privacy (e.g. Dwork et al., 2006), industry-led initiatives have emerged to address these concerns, such as the RAPPOR project (Google, 2014) and the integration of privacy safeguards into Siri (Apple, 2019). In this paper, we further study contextual dynamic pricing under the local differential privacy (LDP) constraint (e.g. Evfimievski et al., 2003; Duchi et al., 2018). LDP provides a decentralized version of differential privacy in the sense that it ensures sensitive personal information remains protected even if the data security of the focal firm is compromised. For /epsilon1 &gt; 0, an /epsilon1 -LDP algorithm masks the sensitive context z t at any time t by ensuring that for any two possible outcomes of the raw data, the probabilities of their privatized data lying in any given measurable set differ by no more than a multiplicative constant exp( /epsilon1 ). Therefore, a smaller /epsilon1 implies higher privacy. Leveraging stochastic gradient descent (SGD), we devise the first dynamic pricing policy in the literature that is /epsilon1 -LDP and achieves a near-optimal regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T//epsilon1 ) under the GLM demand model, therefore characterizing the cost of privacy in parametric contextual

dynamic pricing.

## 1.1 Our contributions

First, we thoroughly study the contextual dynamic pricing problem with a parametric GLM demand function and characterize its near-optimal regret upper bound. In particular, we propose two pricing policies in Section 2 and Section S.3, an ETC algorithm and a confidence bound-type (supCB) algorithm, and show that both achieve a regret of order ˜ O ( √ dT ), with d and T being the dimension of feature vectors and total number of consumers. Thus, we improve the existing upper bound ˜ O ( d √ T ) in the literature (e.g. Ban and Keskin, 2021; Wang et al., 2024; Simchi-Levi and Wang, 2023) by a factor of √ d , suggesting the impact of dimension d to regret can be controlled at a sublinear rate.

Under the condition that the optimal price is unique and an interior point of [ l, u ], a common assumption in the dynamic pricing literature, we show in Section 2.2 that a tuning-free and computationally efficient ETC algorithm can achieve the optimal regret up to a logarithmic term. This is different from the contextual multi-armed bandit (MAB) literature where ETC is in general sub-optimal. The key insight is that due to the structure of the reward function (i.e. the expected revenue), the regret incurred due to estimation error (or more precisely, prediction error) scales with the magnitude of ‖ ̂ θ -θ ‖ 2 instead of ‖ ̂ θ -θ ‖ . We refer to Proposition S.5.2 and its discussion (presented following Theorem 2.2) for more details. We further examine the cause of sub-optimality of the existing upper bound based on the popular MLE-Cycle algorithm (e.g. Broder and Rusmevichientong, 2012; Ban and Keskin, 2021) and Semi-Myopic algorithm (e.g. Keskin and Zeevi, 2014; Den Boer and Zwart, 2014; Simchi-Levi and Wang, 2023) for contextual dynamic pricing, which is under-exploration with respect to dimension d , and suggest a simple fix to make it optimal.

The supCB algorithm proposed in Section S.3 is built upon a confidence bound type algorithm. The key ingredient of supCB is a novel discovery of an intrinsic connection between contextual dynamic pricing and contextual MAB. We show that a carefully designed discretization of the continuous action space [ l, u ] can transform the dynamic pricing problem to an MAB problem with negligible approximation error. Importantly, this connection further helps supCB achieve the near-optimal

O ( √ dT ) regret upper bound under weaker conditions than those imposed in the existing literature.

Third, to demonstrate the optimality of our proposed methods with and without LDP constraints, we further establish the accompanying minimax lower bounds in Section 4. The proofs of lower bounds are based on a novel construction of 2 d indistinguishable demand models. The results, i.e. the Ω( √ dT ) lower bound under the non-private setting and the Ω( d √ T//epsilon1 ) lower bound under the LDP setting, are first time seen in the literature. We note that compared to lower bound constructions for statistical estimation under the LDP setting (e.g. Duchi et al., 2018; Acharya et al., 2024), where data are independent, our lower bound construction is much more involved as data are adaptively collected due to the pricing policy. Extensive numerical results including both simulations and real data applications are conducted in Section 5 to support our theoretical findings and illustrate the

˜ Second, we consider in Section 3 the important and timely setting where the consumer data are subject to LDP constraints. Building upon Duchi et al. (2018) and Chen et al. (2022a), we extend the notion of LDP to the setting of parametric contextual dynamic pricing. Based on SGD in conjunction with the L 2 -ball mechanism in Duchi et al. (2018), we design an efficient ETC-type algorithm that is provably /epsilon1 -LDP and achieves a near-optimal regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T//epsilon1 ). To our best knowledge, this is the first LDP algorithm for contextual dynamic pricing under the GLM demand model and provides the first characterization of the cost of LDP in the task of parametric dynamic pricing. As a byproduct, we study the SGD estimator under /epsilon1 -LDP and carefully analyze the bias-variance trade-off due to an additional truncation step in the L 2 -ball mechanism for handling unbounded gradients. We further show that our policy can be readily modified to cover dynamic pricing under approximate ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP constraints, a less stringent notion than the /epsilon1 -LDP. Moreover, we study dynamic pricing under mixed privacy constraints in Section 3.3, where consumers exhibit heterogeneous privacy preferences and the firm can access raw information from the non-private consumers. We design an ETC-LDP-Mixed algorithm that efficiently combines a private and a non-private SGD to further improve the privacyutility tradeoff by leveraging the public data. We show its regret upper bound seamlessly bridges the ˜ O ( √ dT ) bound in non-private pricing and the ˜ O ( d √ T//epsilon1 ) bound in /epsilon1 -LDP dynamic pricing.

efficiency of the proposed algorithms over existing ones in the contextual dynamic pricing literature.

Literature Review : Three streams of literature are closely related to our paper: dynamic pricing with demand learning, contextual MAB, and differential privacy for online learning. We provide an in-depth literature review on related works in Section S.1 of the supplement.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we formally introduce the problem setup and propose the supCB and ETC algorithms. In Section 3, we further consider contextual dynamic pricing under the additional LDP constraint. Minimax lower bounds are derived in Section 4. We conclude with extensive numerical studies in Section 5.

## 1.2 Notation

For a vector a ∈ R d , denote its Euclidean norm as ‖ a ‖ ; for two square matrices A and B , write A /followsequal B and A /follows B , if A -B is semi-positive definite and positive definite. Denote ⊗ as the Kronecker product. Let ‖ · ‖ op , λ min ( · ) and λ max ( · ) be the L 2 → L 2 -operator norm, the smallest and largest eigenvalues of a matrix. For M /follows 0 and any vector v of compatible dimension, let ‖ v ‖ M -1 = √ v /latticetop M -1 v . For any positive integer K , denote [ K ] = { 1 , . . . , K } . For any vector v and set S , let Π S ( v ) be the L 2 -projection of v onto S . We let O ( · ), o ( · ), Θ( · ) and Ω( · ) denote Landau's Big-O, little-o, Big-Theta and Big-Omega, respectively. The notation ˜ O ( · ) disregards poly-logarithmic factors for O ( · ).

## 2 An improved upper bound on the regret

In this section, we provide an improved upper bound on the regret for contextual dynamic pricing. The detailed problem setup is introduced in Section 2.1, and two algorithms, namely supCB and ETC, are proposed and analyzed in Section 2.2. Section 2.2.2 further discusses the case of adversarial contexts.

## 2.1 Problem setup

We consider a firm, hereafter referred to as the seller , that sells a product to T ∈ N + sequentially arriving consumers. In each time period t ∈ [ T ], the seller observes contextual information featurized as a vector z t ∈ R d , which may include consumer personal information and product characteristics. Upon observing z t , the seller offers a price p t from a compact price interval [ l, u ] ⊆ R + to the consumer and observes a demand y t ∈ R . Note that we do not require the knowledge of T.

Model setup : Denote the covariate as x t = ( z /latticetop t , -p t z /latticetop t ) /latticetop ∈ R 2 d and denote the natural σ -field at time t as F t -1 = σ ( x t , x t -1 , . . . , x 1 , y t -1 , . . . , y 1 ). We assume the demand y t follows a GLM with the probability distribution

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where θ ∗ = ( α ∗/latticetop , β ∗/latticetop ) /latticetop ∈ R 2 d is the unknown model parameter, ψ ( · ) : R ↦→ R is a known function, a ( φ ) is a fixed and known scale parameter and h ( · ) is a known normalizing function. This setting covers important GLMs, including Gaussian, logistic and Poisson regression models.

Conditioning on x t , following (1), it holds that E ( y t | x t ; θ ∗ ) = E ( y t | z t , p t ; θ ∗ ) = ψ ′ ( x /latticetop t θ ∗ ) = ψ ′ ( z /latticetop t α ∗ -z /latticetop t β ∗ p t ), where the inverse function of ψ ′ ( · ) is known as the link function of a GLM. In other words, the consumer demand depends on (i) the intrinsic utility z /latticetop t α ∗ and (ii) the pricing effect ( z /latticetop t β ∗ ) p t , where z /latticetop t β ∗ captures the price elasticity. Define ε t := y t -ψ ′ ( x /latticetop t θ ∗ ) as the error term. We can thus rewrite (1) as y t = ψ ′ ( x /latticetop t θ ∗ ) + ε t and it holds that E ( ε t | x t ) = 0. We impose the following assumptions on the GLM defined in (1), the covariate process { z t } and the model parameter θ ∗ .

Assumption 2.1. (a) { z t } t ∈ [ T ] ⊂ Z ⊆ { z ∈ R d : ‖ z ‖ ≤ 1 } is a sequence of i.i.d. random vectors from an unknown distribution supported on Z . (b) Letting Σ z = E ( z 1 z /latticetop 1 ), we have that λ z := λ min (Σ z ) &gt; 0 . (c) There exist absolute constants u a , u b &gt; 0 such that | z /latticetop α ∗ | ≤ u a and | z /latticetop β ∗ | ≤ u b for any z ∈ Z . (d) Define X := { ( z /latticetop , -pz /latticetop ) /latticetop : z ∈ Z and p ∈ [ l, u ] } . There exists an absolute constant σ &gt; 0 such that for any x ∈ X , we have E { exp( λε t ) | x t = x } ≤ exp( λ 2 σ 2 / 2) for any λ &gt; 0. (e) The function ψ ( · ) is three times continuously differentiable and its second order derivative ψ ′′ ( a ) &gt; 0 for all a ∈ R .

Assumption 2.1 is standard in the dynamic pricing literature, see e.g. Javanmard and Nazerzadeh (2019), Ban and Keskin (2021) and Chen et al. (2022b). Assumption 2.1(a), together with p ∈ [ l, u ], implies that ‖ x ‖ ≤ u x := √ 1 + u 2 for all x ∈ X . Assumption 2.1(b) states that Σ z is of full rank. Note that Assumption 2.1(a) implies that λ z ≤ 1 /d. Assumption 2.1(c) imposes that the intrinsic utility z /latticetop α ∗ and the price sensitivity z /latticetop β ∗ are bounded. Assumption 2.1(d) states that the error term ε t is sub-Gaussian. Assumption 2.1(e) holds for commonly used GLMs and ensures the log-likelihood function to be convex.

Based on Assumption 2.1, we define several absolute constants that will be used later in the paper:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Note that it is easy to see that κ &gt; 0, M ψ 1 , M ψ 2 , M ψ 3 &lt; ∞ , and ( κ, M ψ 1 , M ψ 2 , M ψ 3 ) are absolute constants that only depend on ψ ( · ), l , u , u a and u b in Assumption 2.1.

Pricing policies and performance metric : In dynamic pricing, the key objective is to design an algorithm (i.e. pricing policy) that maximizes the seller's expected revenue. Based on (1), the expected revenue function at any given price p and context z t can be written as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Denote p ∗ t as the optimal price that maximizes r ( p, z t , θ ∗ ).

Denote by Π the family of all price processes { p 1 , . . . , p T } satisfying the condition that p t is F ◦ t -1 -measurable for all t ∈ [ T ], where F ◦ t -1 = σ ( y 1 , · · · , y t -1 , p 1 , · · · , p t -1 , z 1 , · · · , z t ) is the natural filtration generated by the demand, price and context history up to time t -1, together with the context z t . For t = 1, denote F o 0 = σ { z 1 } . In other words, we require the pricing policy to be non-anticipating. Given a pricing policy π ∈ Π, we evaluate its performance using the common notion of regret: the expected revenue loss compared with a clairvoyant that has the perfect knowledge of the model parameter θ ∗ (and thus sets the prices at { p ∗ t } T t =1 ). In particular, with r ( · , · , · ) defined in (3), let the regret be

We aim to design pricing policies that can minimize R π T both in high probability and in expectation.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Non-asymptotic bounds for MLE : For τ ∈ N + , given a sample { ( y t , x t ) } t ∈ [ τ ] , the log-likelihood of the GLM takes the form L ( θ ) = ∑ τ t =1 { y t x /latticetop t θ -ψ ( x /latticetop t θ ) } . Denote V τ = ∑ τ t =1 x t x /latticetop t as the design matrix. By Assumption 2.1(e), L ( θ ) is strictly convex in R 2 d and has a unique maximizer ̂ θ τ (i.e. MLE) given that V τ /follows 0 . Theorem 2.1 provides non-asymptotic performance guarantees for ̂ θ τ .

Theorem 2.1. Suppose that Assumption 2.1(c), (d), (e) hold and { y t } t ∈ [ τ ] are conditionally independent given { x t } t ∈ [ τ ] , i.e. f ( y 1 , · · · , y τ | x 1 , · · · , x τ ) = ∏ τ t =1 f ( y t | x t ; θ ∗ ), where f ( y t | x t ; θ ∗ ) is the

true GLM model in (1). We have that for any δ ∈ (0 , 1) and ς ∈ (0 , 1), with probability at least 1 -δ , it holds that (i) ‖ ̂ θ τ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ ς , provided that λ min ( V τ ) ≥ 3 σ 2 / ( κ 2 ς 2 ) { d + log(1 /δ ) } ; (ii) ( ̂ θ τ -θ ∗ ) /latticetop V τ ( ̂ θ τ -θ ∗ ) ≤ 12 σ 2 /κ 2 { d +log(1 /δ ) } and λ 1 / 2 min ( V τ ) ‖ ̂ θ τ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ √ 3 σ/κ √ d +log(1 /δ ), provided that λ min ( V τ ) ≥ 3 σ 2 /κ 2 { d +log(1 /δ ) } ; and (iii) ∣ ∣ x /latticetop ( ̂ θ τ -θ ∗ ) ∣ ∣ ≤ 3 σ/κ √ log(1 /δ ) ‖ x ‖ V -1 τ for any x ∈ R 2 d , provided that λ min ( V τ ) ≥ 512 M 2 ψ 3 σ 2 /κ 4 { d 2 +log(1 /δ ) } .

Theorem 2.1 states that once the minimum eigenvalue of V τ surpasses some mild thresholds that scale with d and log(1 /δ ), the MLE ̂ θ τ will be consistent as stated in (i) and have controllable prediction and estimation error as stated in (ii) and (iii). Theorem 2.1(i) and (ii) are derived in this work and serve as the theoretical foundation for the ETC algorithm. Theorem 2.1(iii) is adapted from Li et al. (2017) and plays a key role in bounding the regret of the supCB algorithm. Importantly, Theorem 2.1 does not require a compact parameter space Θ and instead allows Θ = R 2 d , due to a nice geometric result for the log-likelihood function of GLM in Chen et al. (1999) (Lemma A therein).

## 2.2 The ETC algorithm

In this section, we propose an explore-then-commit (ETC) algorithm in Algorithm 1, which is tuningfree and computationally efficient, and show that it achieves the optimal regret for dynamic pricing.

## Algorithm 1 The ETC algorithm for dynamic pricing

Input : Total rounds T , price interval [ l, u ], exploration length τ .

a. (Exploration): For t ∈ [ τ ], uniformly choose p t ∈ [ l, u ], record y t and x t = ( z /latticetop t , -p t z /latticetop t ) /latticetop into the price experiment set Ψ.

b. (Estimation): Obtain MLE ̂ θ based on observations in Ψ. c. (Exploitation): For t = τ +1 , . . . , T , offer the greedy price at ̂ p ∗ t = p ∗ ( ̂ θ, z t ) = arg max p ∈ [ l,u ] r ( p, z t , ̂ θ ).

ETC employs a simple two-stage structure: in the exploration stage, it conducts τ rounds of price experiments where prices are randomly drawn from [ l, u ]; it then obtains an MLE ̂ θ based on the sample collected and switches to the exploitation stage, where it uses ̂ θ to set prices for the rest T -τ rounds. We remark that ETC is a standard algorithm in the bandit literature (e.g. Lattimore and Szepesv´ ari, 2020) and is recently used in semi-parametric dynamic pricing in Fan et al. (2024). Importantly, ETC only requires a single computation of MLE, which is highly efficient.

Denote µ p and σ 2 p as the mean and variance of the uniform distribution on [ l, u ] and denote Σ p = [1 , -µ p ; -µ p , µ 2 p + σ 2 p ] ∈ R 2 × 2 . It is easy to see that µ p = ( u + l ) / 2, σ 2 p = ( u -l ) 2 / 12 and

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Write Σ x = Σ p ⊗ Σ z , which is the covariance matrix of the feature x t = ( z /latticetop t , -z /latticetop t p t ) /latticetop during the pure exploration phase. By the property of the Kronecker product and Assumption 2.1(b), it holds that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which quantifies the information gain from each consumer during price exploration. We proceed by introducing Assumption 2.2, which is needed for theoretical guarantees of ETC.

Assumption 2.2. There exists an absolute constant ς o &gt; 0 such that for any θ ∈ { θ : ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ ς o } and any context vector z ∈ ¯ Z , with ¯ Z being the closure of Z , it holds that the optimal price p ∗ = arg max p ∈ [ l,u ] r ( p, z, θ ) is unique and is an interior point of the price range [ l, u ].

We remark that Assumption 2.2 is a mild assumption commonly used in the dynamic pricing literature under various settings (e.g. Broder and Rusmevichientong, 2012; Javanmard and Nazerzadeh, 2019; Ban and Keskin, 2021; Chen and Gallego, 2021; Lei et al., 2023; Fan et al., 2024). In particular, it requires that the optimal price p ∗ is unique for all context vector and all θ within an arbitrarily small neighborhood of θ ∗ . To understand Assumption 2.2, consider a linear demand model. We have that the optimal price is unique over R and is p ∗ = z /latticetop α/ (2 z /latticetop β ) for any z and θ = ( α /latticetop , β /latticetop ) /latticetop . Therefore, given that the true price elasticity z /latticetop β ∗ is lower bounded by an absolute constant c b such that z /latticetop β ∗ ≥ c b &gt; 0 for all z ∈ ¯ Z (i.e. all consumers are sensitive to prices), one can always find a sufficiently large interval [ l, u ] and sufficiently small ς o such that Assumption 2.2 holds. By simple algebra, the same holds under a logistic demand model. In the following, without loss of generality, we assume ς o ∈ (0 , 1].

An important implication of Assumption 2.2 is that the optimal price p ∗ is a Lipschitz continuous function of θ over ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ ς o uniformly for all z ∈ Z , as stated in Proposition S.5.2 of the supplement. In other words, when the parameter estimation error ‖ ̂ θ -θ ∗ ‖ is sufficiently small, its impact on the estimation error of the optimal price is Lipschitz. Such a Lipschitz continuity property is explicitly

assumed in the literature (e.g. Broder and Rusmevichientong, 2012; Ban and Keskin, 2021), while we give a formal proof under mild conditions. As will be discussed later, this Lipschitz continuity property of the optimal price function plays a key role in the theoretical guarantees for ETC.

Theorem 2.2. Suppose Assumptions 2.1 and 2.2 hold. For any δ ∈ (0 , 1 / 3), set τ = √ Td log(1 /δ ). For any T satisfying

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

we have that, with probability at least 1 -3 δ , the regret of ETC is upper bounded by R T ≤ B E 3 · √ dT log(1 /δ ), where B E 1 , B E 2 , B E 3 &gt; 0 are absolute constants that only depend on quantities in Assumptions 2.1 and 2.2. In particular, setting δ = 1 /T , we have that E ( R T ) /lessorsimilar √ dT log( T ).

The details of B E 1 , B E 2 and B E 3 can be found in the proof of Theorem 2.2 in the supplement. Remarkably, despite its simplicity, ETC achieves a near-optimal regret ˜ O ( √ dT ) for sufficiently large T . The key intuition lies in a second-order structure of the revenue function due to Assumption 2.2 and Proposition S.5.2. In particular, for the regret incurred in the exploitation stage, we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the equality follows from a second-order Taylor expansion of the revenue function r ( ̂ p ∗ t , z t , θ ∗ ) around p ∗ t (with p + t between p ∗ t and ̂ p ∗ t ) and the fact that p ∗ t ∈ ( l, u ) is the maximizer of the revenue function, and the inequality follows from the fact that | ∂ 2 r ( p, z t , θ ∗ ) /∂p 2 | &lt; C r for an absolute constant C r (Lemma S.5.2) and the Lipschitz continuity of the optimal price (Proposition S.5.2). In the proof of Theorem 2.2, we show that the last term in (8) is related to the squared prediction error of ̂ θ , which can be well controlled using results in Theorem 2.1(ii) and a matrix concentration inequality. Importantly, this helps make the regret bound in Theorem 2.2 independent of λ z .

This indicates that the regret in (8) is of order ˜ O ( dT/τ ) when the exploration set is of length τ . This is the key for the optimality of ETC. Without Assumption 2.2, the arguments in (8) will not hold and we can only control the regret at the order of ˜ O ( dT/ √ τ ) using the Lipschitz property of the

revenue function, leading to a sub-optimal regret and making supCB in Section S.3 necessary.

Unknown T . When T is unknown, we couple ETC with the standard doubling trick (e.g. Auer et al., 1995). We summarize the algorithm, named ETC-Doubling, in Algorithm 2. For a given dimension d , denote T ∗ d as the minimum T such that condition (7) holds with δ = 1 /T .

## Algorithm 2 The ETC-Doubling algorithm for dynamic pricing

Input : Price interval [ l, u ], Ψ = ∅ .

k

rounds, randomly choose into the price experiment set Ψ.

c. (Estimation): Obtain MLE ̂ θ based on observations in Ψ. d. (Exploitation): For the rest E k -τ k rounds, offer the greedy price at p ∗ ( ̂ θ, z t ) based on ̂ θ . end for

∈

-

Theorem 2.3. Suppose that Assumptions 2.1 and 2.2 hold. For any T satisfying T ≥ T ∗ 2 d /d , with probability at least 1 -6 / √ dT , the regret of ETC-Doubling is upper bounded by R T ≤ B E 4 √ dT log( T ), where B E 4 &gt; 0 is an absolute constant only depending on quantities in Assumptions 2.1 and 2.2.

Remark 2.1 (Comparison with MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic) . Two popular algorithms in the dynamic pricing literature are the MLE-Cycle algorithm (e.g. Broder and Rusmevichientong, 2012; Ban and Keskin, 2021) and the Semi-Myopic type algorithm (e.g. Keskin and Zeevi, 2014; Simchi-Levi and Wang, 2023), both achieving a regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T ). The key idea of MLE-Cycle is to divide the horizon N + into cycles. The c th cycle, c ∈ N + , is of length c + k , where k ∈ N + is a constant . Within each cycle, the first k rounds are used for price experiments and the next c rounds for price exploitation. The key idea of Semi-Myopic is to run a greedy dynamic pricing policy but further inject a controlled deviation (in particular, of size κt -1 / 4 for some constant κ &gt; 0) into the greedily estimated optimal price. In Sections S.5.4 and S.5.5 of the supplement, we show the sub-optimality of MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic is due to under-exploration, i.e. the constant k and κ do not scale with the dimension d optimally. Moreover, we show that via a simple modification that boosts exploration, both algorithms can achieve the optimal regret of order ˜ O ( √ dT ). On the other hand, due to their design, MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic require the computation of O ( √ T ) and O ( T ) many MLEs, respectively, while ETC-

Doubling only requires O (log 2 ( T )). In Section 5, we show via extensive numerical experiments that ETC-Doubling achieves better statistical and computational efficiency in finite-sample.

## 2.2.1 The supCB Algorithm

In Section S.3 of the supplement, we further propose supCB, which achieves a near-optimal ˜ O ( √ dT ) regret under assumptions weaker than the ones required in the existing dynamic pricing literature. In particular, it does not require Assumption 2.2, which imposes uniqueness of the true optimal price.

The supCB algorithm is a more complex version of the widely used upper confidence bound (UCB) algorithm and is inspired by algorithms designed for the classical contextual MAB problem (e.g. Auer, 2002; Chu et al., 2011; Li et al., 2017). The basis of supCB is a novel discovery of an intrinsic connection between contextual dynamic pricing and contextual MAB. By discretizing the one-dimensional continuous action space [ l, u ] with K equally-spaced price points, we can approximately treat the contextual dynamic pricing problem as a contextual MAB with K arms. Importantly, we show that regret due to the approximation error of such discretization can be controlled at O ( T/K ) . It is wellknown that the optimal regret of contextual MAB with K arms is ˜ O ( √ dT log( K )). Therefore, setting K = O ( √ T/d ) will intuitively provide a desired ˜ O ( √ dT ) upper bound for contextual dynamic pricing. We refer to Section S.3 of the supplement for more details of supCB.

Both supCB and ETC achieve the near-optimal regret ˜ O ( √ dT ), with ETC offering a slightly better rate than supCB by a log( T ) factor under an additional mild Assumption 2.2. However, ETC is much simpler in its design with better computational efficiency. In particular, ETC only requires a single computation of MLE, while supCB requires the computation of at least O ( T ) many MLE. Indeed, extensive numerical experiments in Section S.2.1 of the supplement indicate that ETC is superior than supCB in both statistical and computational efficiency, where ETC gives an expected regret 3-4 times lower than supCB while using only 1/50 of its computational time. We refer to Section S.2.1 for details. Therefore, in practice, we recommend ETC over supCB.

## 2.2.2 Dynamic pricing under adversarial contexts

In this paper, we focus on dynamic pricing under stochastic contexts, where the feature vector { z t } is a sequence of i.i.d. random vectors with full-rank covariance satisfying Assumption 2.1(a)-(b). To

our knowledge, all existing works in dynamic pricing under parametric GLM demand models assume stochastic contexts, with the exception of Wang et al. (2024), where the only assumption on { z t } is that it is a sequence of bounded vectors (i.e. ‖ z t ‖ ≤ 1), namely the adversarial contexts.

In general, for an online learning problem, the upper bound algorithms and minimax rates can be substantially different under stochastic and adversarial contexts. Nevertheless, for a more comprehensive view, we provide a brief study of dynamic pricing under adversarial contexts in Sections S.3.1 and S.4 of the supplement. We give an overview of the results below.

Note that due to the adversarial contexts, the MLE ̂ θ τ may not be well-defined as the design matrix V τ can be rank-deficient. Thus, we instead rely on the regularized MLE ˜ θ τ , which is the unique minimizer of the regularized (negative) log-likelihood -L ( θ ) + ‖ θ ‖ 2 . In particular, based on ˜ θ τ , we propose a modified supCB algorithm in Section S.3.1 and show that, under an additional assumption on the link function ψ ( · ) of the GLM (which is satisfied by the linear demand model), it achieves the near-optimal ˜ O ( √ dT ) regret upper bound under adversarial contexts.

Furthermore, in Section S.4, we propose a novel UCB algorithm that works for a general GLM demand model under adversarial contexts and matches the ˜ O ( d √ T ) regret upper bound in Wang et al. (2024). Notably, our algorithm requires weaker assumptions and is computationally more efficient than the one in Wang et al. (2024). The key novelty of our proposed UCB algorithm lies in its design of the upper confidence bound for the true revenue function. In particular, our upper confidence bound takes a closed-form by leveraging the structure of the revenue function r ( p, z t , θ ∗ ) and the convexity of the link function ψ ( · ) of GLM (i.e. Assumption 2.1(e), which makes ψ ′ ( · ) a strictly increasing function).

We refer to the supplement for more detailed results of our proposed algorithms, theoretical guarantees, and numerical experiments that showcase the efficiency (in terms of both regret and computational time) of our algorithm compared to the algorithms in Wang et al. (2024).

## 3 Contextual dynamic pricing under LDP constraints

As discussed in the introduction, there is a growing trend for implementing privacy preserving practice in business operations where personal information is used. This motivates us to further study

contextual dynamic pricing within the framework of LDP. We first introduce the notion of LDP for contextual dynamic pricing, then propose the ETC-LDP algorithm in Section 3.1 and establish its regret upper bound in Section 3.2. In Section 3.3, we further extend our study to contextual dynamic pricing under mixed privacy constraints, which bridges non-private and private dynamic pricing.

For t ∈ [ T ], let s t = ( z t , y t , p t ) be the raw data observed at time t . We follow the formulation of LDP in dynamic pricing put forward by the seminal work Chen et al. (2022a), where the privacy-preserving policy consists of a two-part structure that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

with A t being the pricing strategy and Q t being the privacy mechanism. Here, w t denotes the privatized statistic based on the raw data s t and we denote w &lt;t = ( w 1 , · · · , w t -1 ) with w &lt; 0 := ∅ .

The pricing strategy A t ( z t , w &lt;t ) utilizes the true context z t of the t th customer and the privatized information w &lt;t from previous time periods. Note that while the raw context z t is utilized for pricing purposes, it is never retained or stored by the seller. In particular, the personalized price p t is computed based on z t locally on the customer's device and the only data passed to the seller is the privatized w t . The privatization mechanism Q t ( s t , w &lt;t ) is used to transform the raw data of the t th customer, s t , into a privatized statistic w t . Note that Q t only utilizes the privatized statistics w &lt;t and thus maintains privacy for previously encountered consumers. We remark that the seller does not store raw customer data s t , but only the privatized statistic w t is retained for future pricing. Following Duchi et al. (2018) and Chen et al. (2022a), we have the below definition.

Definition 3.1 ( /epsilon1 -LDP for dynamic pricing) . For any /epsilon1 &gt; 0, a policy π = { Q t , A t } T t =1 satisfies /epsilon1 -LDP if for any t ∈ [ T ], w &lt;t and s t , s ′ t , we have Q t ( W | s t , w &lt;t ) ≤ e /epsilon1 Q t ( W | s ′ t , w &lt;t ), for any measurable set W .

We remark that our theoretical results hold for any /epsilon1 ∈ (0 , b ], where b is an absolute constant. For simplicity, we assume in the following that /epsilon1 ∈ (0 , 1] in all of our theoretical results regarding LDP.

## 3.1 The ETC-LDP algorithm

Algorithm 3 presents the proposed ETC-LDP algorithm for privacy-preserving contextual dynamic pricing, with an L 2 -ball mechanism subroutine (Duchi et al., 2018) in Algorithm 4. The high-level structure of ETC-LDP is the same as the ETC algorithm in Section 2.2 for non-private dynamic pricing. In particular, it partitions the entire horizon into an exploration phase and an exploitation phase. The exploration phase is used for private parameter estimation while in the exploitation phase, the seller uses a greedy approach to set optimal prices based on the estimated model parameter.

Unlike ETC where the seller can simply conduct a single maximum likelihood estimation after collecting all raw data { s t } t ∈ [ τ ] in the exploration phase, an alternative estimator is needed for ETCLDP to meet the LDP constraint. In particular, leveraging the sequential nature of the LDP notion and dynamic pricing, we employ a stochastic gradient descent (SGD) based privacy-preserving estimation procedure and integrate it into the exploration phase of ETC-LDP (i.e. Steps a1-a3 in Algorithm 3).

Algorithm 3 The ETC-LDP algorithm for privacy-preserving dynamic pricing Input : Total rounds T , price interval [ l, u ], parameter space Θ , privacy parameter /epsilon1 , exploration length τ , learning rate of SGD ζ l , gradient truncation parameter C g a. (Exploration) Randomly sample an initial estimator ̂ θ 0 from Θ. for t ∈ [ τ ] do a1. (Experiment): uniformly choose p t from [ l, u ], observe raw data s t = ( z t , y t , p t ) . a2. (Privatization): compute the truncated gradient g [ C ] t = g [ C ] t ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t ) = Π B Cg [ g ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t )] , with g ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t ) defined in (11); run the L 2 -ball subroutine on g [ C ] t with parameter C g and /epsilon1 , and obtain w t = W ( g [ C ] t ). a3. (SGD): update estimation via ̂ θ t = Π Θ [ ̂ θ t -1 + η t w t ] with step size η t = ( ζ l t ) -1 . end for b. (Exploitation) for t = τ +1 , · · · , T do offer the greedy price at p t = p ∗ ( ̂ θ τ , z t ) = arg max p ∈ [ l,u ] r ( p, z t , ̂ θ τ ) based on ̂ θ τ . end for

We now give a detailed explanation of Steps a1-a3. Step a1 creates a new data point s t = ( z t , p t , y t ) based on price experiments. Define the log-likelihood function and its gradient based on { s t } as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Algorithm 4 The L 2 -ball mechanism (Duchi et al., 2018). L 2 -ball ( g, C g , /epsilon1 )

Input : Raw data g ∈ R 2 d , upper bound C g such that ‖ g ‖ ≤ C g , privacy parameter /epsilon1 .

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

1. Generate ˜ X = (2 b -1) g where b ∼ Bernoulli(1 / 2 + ‖ g ‖ / (2 C g )). 2. Generate random vector W( g ) via

where r /epsilon1,d = √ π e /epsilon1 +1 e /epsilon1 -1 d Γ ( d + 1 2 ) Γ( d +1) and Γ( · ) is the Gamma function.

Output : w = W( g ).

respectively. The update direction of the standard SGD will be g ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t ) given the current estimator ̂ θ t -1 . However, to preserve privacy, we need to privatize g ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t ), done in Step a2 via the L 2 -ball mechanism (Duchi et al., 2018). In particular, Step a2 first computes a truncated gradient g [ C ] t by projecting g ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t ) to the ball B C g ⊂ R 2 d , as the L 2 -ball mechanism requires a bounded input. It then runs the L 2 -ball subroutine and obtains the privatized gradient w t . Step a3 updates the estimator via θ t = Π Θ [ θ t -1 + η t w t ] with a step size η t = 1 / ( ζ l t ) that scales with 1 /t.

̂ ̂ After exploration, we obtain the privacy-preserving estimator ̂ θ τ , which is then used in the exploitation phase. In Proposition S.6.1 of the supplement, we show that ETC-LDP is indeed /epsilon1 -LDP.

## 3.2 Regret upper bound of ETC-LDP

We start with a mild modeling assumption.

Assumption 3.1. (a) The parameter space Θ is compact and satisfies that θ ∗ ∈ Θ ⊆ { θ : ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ c θ } , for some absolute constant c θ &gt; 0. (b) There exist absolute constants c λ 1 , c λ 2 &gt; 0 such that c λ 1 /d ≤ λ z ≤ ¯ λ z ≤ c λ 2 /d , where recall λ z = λ min (Σ z ) and ¯ λ z = λ max (Σ z ), with { z t } satisfying Assumption 2.1 and Σ z = E ( z 1 z /latticetop 1 ).

Assumptions 2.1(c) and 3.1(a) together ensure that { x /latticetop θ : x ∈ X and θ ∈ Θ } is bounded. Define M ∗ ψ 1 = sup ( x,θ ) ∈X⊗ Θ | ψ ′ ( x /latticetop θ ) | ∨ 1 and κ ∗ = inf ( x,θ ) ∈X⊗ Θ ψ ′′ ( x /latticetop θ ) ∧ 1. By simple algebra and (6), we have that the population log-likelihood function l ( θ ) := E [ l ( θ ; s t )] in the exploration phase of ETC-LDP is φ z -strongly concave over Θ with φ z := L p κ ∗ λ z . This serves as the foundation for the theoretical analysis of the SGD estimator ̂ θ τ . Note that if we assume ‖ θ ∗ ‖ ≤ c for some absolute

constant c , Assumption 3.1(a) then holds by setting Θ = { θ : ‖ θ ‖ ≤ c } and c θ = 2 c .

Assumption 3.1(b) imposes the mild condition that λ z /equivasymptotic ¯ λ z /equivasymptotic 1 /d . This essentially requires z t to have a balanced covariance matrix, which is a standard assumption in the statistical regression literature. Note that the 1 /d factor is due to the fact that ‖ z t ‖ ≤ 1 as in Assumption 2.1(a). Theorem 3.1 gives a high-probability upper bound on the estimation error for the private SGD estimator ̂ θ τ in the exploration phase of ETC-LDP, serving as the basis for bounding the regret of ETC-LDP.

Theorem 3.1. Suppose that Assumptions 2.1 and 3.1(a) hold. For any τ ≥ 4, set C g = 2 √ 1 + u 2 ( M ∗ ψ 1 + σ √ log( τ )) and ζ l = c l φ z with some c l ∈ (0 , 1], where recall φ z = L p κ ∗ λ z . We have that, for any /epsilon1 &gt; 0 and 0 &lt; δ &lt; { e ∨ log( τ ) } -1 , with probability at least 1 -δ log( τ ), it holds that ‖ ̂ θ τ -θ ∗ ‖ 2 ≤ B L 1 d log(1 /δ ) log( τ ) / ( c 2 l λ 2 z /epsilon1 2 τ ), where B L 1 &gt; 0 is an absolute constant that only depends on quantities in Assumptions 2.1 and 3.1(a).

We remark that our private SGD estimator for the GLM is adapted from a similar procedure in Duchi et al. (2018) without the truncation mechanism. Assuming the boundedness of the gradient, Duchi et al. (2018) provide an asymptotic estimation error bound (in our notation) such that E {‖ ̂ θ τ -θ ∗ ‖ 2 } /lessorsimilar d/ ( λ 2 z /epsilon1 2 τ ), and show its asymptotic minimax optimality. In contrast, we provide a non-asymptotic high-probability bound for the private SGD estimator via substantially different technical arguments adapted from the standard SGD literature (Rakhlin et al., 2011). As we allow an unbounded gradient, our result requires a careful analysis of the additional bias-variance trade-off due to truncation. Our result matches the one in Duchi et al. (2018), up to logarithmic terms, with log(1 /δ ) stemming from the high probability nature of our bound and log( τ ) from the truncation bias.

For the case where the minimum eigenvalue λ z /equivasymptotic d -1 , the MLE in Theorem 2.1(ii) and the private SGD in Theorem 3.1 achieve high-probability upper bounds, up to logarithmic factors, ‖ ̂ θ MLE τ -θ ∗ ‖ 2 /lessorsimilar d 2 /τ and ‖ ̂ θ SGD τ -θ ∗ ‖ 2 /lessorsimilar d 3 / ( /epsilon1 2 τ ). This suggests that the LDP constraint shrinks the effective sample size from τ to τ/epsilon1 2 /d , a phenomenon repeatedly observed in the LDP literature (e.g. Li et al., 2023).

Remark 3.1 (Learning rate ζ l ) . A key tuning parameter of the private SGD estimator is the learning rate ζ l , which regulates the step size η t = 1 / ( ζ l t ) . Inline with the standard SGD literature (e.g.

Rakhlin et al., 2011), we require ζ l = c l φ z for some c l ∈ (0 , 1] to achieve the optimal error bound. The term φ z = L p κ ∗ λ z is the strong-concavity parameter of the population log-likelihood function and in general unknown. However, if the order of λ z is known, e.g. λ z /equivasymptotic 1 /d as in Assumption 3.1(b), one can set c l = 1 / loglog( τ ) and replace φ z by its order 1 /d in ζ l . It is easy to see that this ζ l will be valid for all sufficiently large τ and the estimation error of SGD will only be inflated by a poly-logarithmic factor. Therefore, for simplicity, we set ζ l = φ z in the regret analysis of ETC-LDP in Theorem 3.2.

Theorem 3.2. Suppose that Assumptions 2.1, 2.2 and 3.1 hold. For any /epsilon1 &gt; 0 and 0 &lt; δ &lt; { e ∨ log( T ) } -1 , set the learning rate ζ l = φ z , the exploration length τ = d √ T log( T ) log(1 /δ ) //epsilon1 and the truncation parameter C g = 2 √ 1 + u 2 ( M ∗ ψ 1 + σ √ log( τ )). For any T satisfying

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

we have that, with probability at least 1 -δ log( T ), the regret of ETC-LDP is upper bounded by R T ≤ B L 3 d √ log( T ) log(1 /δ ) T//epsilon1 , where B L 2 , B L 3 &gt; 0 are absolute constants only depending on quantities in Assumptions 2.1, 2.2 and 3.1. In particular, setting δ = 1 /T , we have that E ( R T ) /lessorsimilar /epsilon1 -1 d √ T log( T ) .

Theorem 3.2 suggests that ETC-LDP achieves a regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T//epsilon1 ). Compared to ETC, which achieves a regret of order ˜ O ( √ dT ), we see that the LDP constraint inflates the regret by √ d//epsilon1 , matching the phenomenon for the private SGD estimator when compared with MLE.

In Theorems 3.1 and 3.2, we require a diverging C g to control the bias due to truncation of the gradient. For GLMs where y t is bounded, C g can be made tighter as a constant. For example, we can set C g = √ 1 + u 2 for the logistic regression. This would improve the estimation error (and hence regret) by a poly-logarithmic factor. Same with supCB and ETC, we can further extend ETC-LDP to the case where T is unknown with the doubling trick. We omit the details to conserve space.

Remark 3.2 (Dynamic pricing under ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP) . A relaxation of /epsilon1 -LDP is ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP, where one further allows a ∆ ∈ (0 , 1] probability of privacy leakage (see Definition S.6.1 for more details). In Section S.6.5 of the supplement, we propose an ETC-LDP-Approx algorithm for dynamic pricing under ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP and show that it achieves the same order of regret upper bound as ETC-LDP under /epsilon1 -LDP (up to a O ( √ log(1 . 25 / ∆)) term), a common phenomenon in the LDP literature (Duchi et al.,

2018). The key modification is to replace the L 2 -ball privacy mechanism of ETC-LDP with a Gaussian mechanism of a carefully calibrated variance. Technically, we extend the uniform concentration inequality for martingale difference sequences in Rakhlin et al. (2011) from bounded random variables to random variables with certain moment conditions (e.g. sub-Gaussian). This inequality is of independent interest (see Lemma S.6.2). Numerical experiments are further conducted to show the similar performance by ETC-LDP and ETC-LDP-Approx in finite-sample. Thus, in practice, we recommend ETC-LDP as it offers stronger privacy guarantees. We refer to Section S.6.5 for more details.

## 3.3 Dynamic pricing under mixed privacy constraints

The ETC-LDP algorithm and its theoretical guarantees, as studied in Sections 3.1 and 3.2, are designed for the scenario where all users have the same level of privacy guarantees. However, in reality, consumers may exhibit heterogeneous preferences towards the trade-off between privacy and personalization, or simply different attitudes towards privacy. To accommodate this, firms often offer consumers some flexibility regarding their privacy levels, such as cookies consent banners, mobile apps permissions, social media privacy settings, search engine privacy modes and e-commerce personalization preferences, where users typically are given the choices to opt in or out of certain privacy protection mechanism. This flexibility and heterogeneity present both opportunities and challenges in statistical privacy. For example, recent research has explored leveraging public data in privacy analysis to improve utility and developing personalized learning algorithms that adapt to individual privacy preferences; see Cummings et al. (2024) and references therein.

This motivate us to further extend our study to dynamic pricing under mixed privacy constraints. In particular, we consider the scenario where arriving consumers exhibit two levels of privacy preferences: non-private, where they are willing to share raw information s t = ( z t , y t , p t ) with the firm, and private, where they require an /epsilon1 -LDP protection of s t . More specifically, we assume that the consumer's privacy preference is encoded by a random variable m t ∈ { 0 , 1 } where m t = 0 indicates a private consumer and m t = 1 indicates a non-private consumer, and there is an unknown mixture probability p ∗ ∈ [0 , 1] such that each arriving consumer has p ∗ probability of being non-private and

1 -p ∗ probability of being private with /epsilon1 -LDP. In addition, { m t } is an i.i.d. process across consumers. Same as before, we assume /epsilon1 ∈ (0 , 1] for notational simplicity and remark that all results hold for any /epsilon1 ∈ (0 , b ], where b is an absolute constant.

Intuitively, the presence of non-private consumers (regulated by p ∗ ) can help improve model estimation for θ ∗ and thus attain a lower regret compared to dynamic pricing under pure /epsilon1 -LDP constraints (i.e. p ∗ ≡ 0) in Section 3.1. To achieve this, we propose the ETC-LDP-Mixed algorithm in Algorithm 5, which is adapted from ETC-LDP in Algorithm 3 with several important modifications to effectively utilize the information from both private and non-private consumers.

First, thanks to the information from the non-private consumers, we are able to explore less than ETC-LDP. In particular, given p ∗ ∈ (0 , 1), the optimal exploration length now takes the form

Recall as discussed following Theorem 3.1, /epsilon1 2 /d can be viewed as the effective sample size of an /epsilon1 -LDP private consumer. Since /epsilon1 2 /d ≤ 1, τ ( p ∗ ) is a decreasing function of p ∗ . That is, having more non-private consumers (thus higher quality data) leads to a shorter exploration length. Note that we have τ (1) = √ dT log( T ) for p ∗ = 1 and τ (0) = d √ T log( T ) //epsilon1 for p ∗ = 0, which recover the optimal order of exploration length for ETC in Theorem 2.2 under the non-private case and for ETC-LDP in Theorem 3.2 under the /epsilon1 -LDP private case, respectively. Therefore, τ ( p ∗ ) is intuitive and bridges private and non-private dynamic pricing seamlessly.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

In practice, p ∗ may be unknown, which further motivates the two-stage exploration design for ETC-LDP-Mixed. In Stage I exploration, which lasts for τ 1 = √ dT rounds, we estimate ̂ p ∗ from the consumer privacy preference data { m t } τ 1 t =1 while conducting parameter estimation of θ ∗ via private SGD. The Stage II exploration is then run for τ ( ̂ p ∗ ) -τ 1 rounds, where we estimate θ ∗ via both a private SGD and a non-private SGD to effectively utilize the information from both private and non-private consumers.

In particular, due to the /epsilon1 -LDP constraint, the private SGD needs to be updated right after each private consumer arrives, while we can conduct the non-private SGD at the end of Stage II exploration,

as the non-private data can be shared with the firm and stored in its database S . Same as the logic of τ ( p ∗ ) in (13), we adjust the k th step size of the non-private SGD (step B3 in Algorithm 5) as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

```
̂ Algorithm 5 The ETC-LDP-Mixed algorithm for dynamic pricing under mixed privacy constraints Input : Total rounds T , price interval [ l, u ], parameter space Θ , privacy parameter /epsilon1 , learning rate of SGD ζ l , gradient truncation parameter C g Initialization : Randomly sample an initial estimator ̂ θ 0 from Θ. Set S = ∅ . Set τ 1 = √ dT . A. (Stage I Exploration) for t ∈ { 1 , 2 , · · · , τ 1 } do A1. uniformly choose p t from [ l, u ], observe privacy preference m t and raw data s t = ( z t , y t , p t ). if m t = 0 (i.e. private consumer) then A2. run a2-a3 in Alg. 3 with step size η t = 1 / ( ζ l ( t -|S| )) to update ̂ θ t . /triangleright (Private SGD) else (i.e. m t = 1, non-private consumer) A3. set S = S ∪ { s t } and set ̂ θ t = ̂ θ t -1 . /triangleright (Store non-private data) end if end for B. (Stage II Exploration) B1. set ̂ p ∗ = |S| /τ 1 and τ 2 = τ ( ̂ p ∗ ) with τ ( p ∗ ) defined in (13). /triangleright (Estimated explr. length) for t ∈ { τ 1 +1 , · · · , τ 2 } do B2. run step A1-A3 for the t th consumer. end for for k ∈ { 1 , 2 , · · · , |S|} do /triangleright (Non-private SGD on S ) B3. set step size η k = ζ -1 l (( τ 2 -|S| ) /epsilon1 2 /d + k ) -1 and update ̂ θ τ 2 = Π Θ [ ̂ θ τ 2 + η k g ( ̂ θ τ 2 ; s k )] with s k being the k th data in S and g ( θ ; s ) defined in (11). end for C. (Exploitation) for t ∈ { τ 2 +1 , · · · , T } do offer the greedy price at p t = p ∗ ( ̂ θ τ 2 , z t ) = arg max p ∈ [ l,u ] r ( p, z t , ̂ θ τ 2 ) based on ̂ θ τ 2 . end for
```

where ζ l is the learning rate and ( τ ( ̂ p ∗ ) -|S| ) /epsilon1 2 /d accounts for the effective sample size of the private SGD estimate based on the τ ( p ∗ ) -|S| private consumers in the exploration phase.

Theorem 3.3. Suppose the conditions in Theorem 3.2 hold. For any /epsilon1 &gt; 0 and 0 &lt; δ &lt; { e ∨ log( T ) } -1 , set the learning rate ζ l and truncation parameter C g same as in Theorem 3.2. For any T satisfying T ≥ B M 2 · { ( λ -4 z /epsilon1 -2 log( T ) log(1 /δ )) ∨ ( d/epsilon1 -4 log 2 ( T )) } , with probability at least 1 -δ log( T ), the regret of ETC-LDP-Mixed is upper bounded by R T ≤ B M 3 √ log( T ) log(1 /δ ) dT/ √ p ∗ +(1 -p ∗ ) /epsilon1 2 /d , where B M 2 , B M 3 &gt; 0 are absolute constants only depending on quantities in Assumptions 2.1, 2.2 and 3.1.

Note that Theorem 3.3 recovers and thus bridges the rate of regret upper bounds for non-private

dynamic pricing in Theorem 2.2 and /epsilon1 -LDP private dynamic pricing in Theorem 3.2, by setting p ∗ = 1 and p ∗ = 0, respectively. To our knowledge, this is the first time such result is seen in the literature. In particular, when p ∗ ∈ (0 , 1], ETC-LDP-Mixed can effectively leverage the non-private consumers and therefore achieve an improved privacy-utility trade-off. One key ingredient of Theorem 3.3 is a novel non-asymptotic bound on the estimation error of θ ∗ that combines a private SGD and a non-private SGD, which is detailed in Lemma S.6.4 of the supplement and can be of independent interest. Extensive numerical experiments in Section 5 suggest that notable improvement in regret can be observed even with a small p ∗ such as 0 . 05 or 0 . 1.

## 4 Minimax lower bounds on the regret

In this section, we verify the optimality of our proposed supCB, ETC and ETC-LDP algorithms.

Theorem 4.1 (Non-private lower bound) . Let P be the collection of distributions satisfying the GLM model in (1), Assumptions 2.1 and 2.2, and Π be the set of all non-anticipating policies. There exists an absolute constant c &gt; 0 such that inf π ∈ Π sup v ∈P E π v R T ≥ c √ dT , for all T ≥ 4 d 2 log( T ).

˜ ˜ Theorem 4.1 shows that both supCB and ETC achieve the minimax optimal regret, up to logarithmic factors. The sub-linear dependence on the dimensionality d is the first time seen in the contextual dynamic pricing literature and extends the Ω( √ T ) lower bound for contextual-free dynamic pricing in Broder and Rusmevichientong (2012). Unlike Broder and Rusmevichientong (2012), which connects the regret with the error of a hypothesis testing problem between two highly indistinguishable demand models and applies Le Cam's lemma, our technical arguments are substantially different and more involved. In particular, to capture the effect of the dimension d , we construct 2 d demand models that can be grouped into pairs, where each pair is highly indistinguishable along one dimension of the context vector. We connect the regret with the error of a multiple-classification problem and establish the lower bound based on Assouad's method. We remark that due to adaptivity of the data (i.e. { y t , z t , p t } generated by the pricing policy are not i.i.d.), our arguments are more involved than standard applications of Assouad's method in lower bounds for statistical estimation problems.

Theorem 4.2 (Private lower bound) . Let P be the collection of distributions satisfying the GLM

model in (1), Assumptions 2.1, 2.2 and 3.1, and Π( /epsilon1 ) be the set of all non-anticipating /epsilon1 -LDP policies as defined in Definition 3.1. There exists an absolute constant ˜ c &gt; 0 such that inf π ∈ Π( /epsilon1 ) sup v ∈P E π v R T ≥ cd √ T//epsilon1 , for all T ≥ c ′ · d 4 /epsilon1 -2 log( T ), where c ′ &gt; 0 is an absolute constant.

˜ ˜ ˜ Theorem 4.2 shows that ETC-LDP achieves the minimax optimal regret, up to logarithmic factors. Compared with Theorem 4.1, the regret lower bound under LDP inflates by a factor of √ d//epsilon1 , and the condition on T is more stringent, echoing observations in the privacy literature (e.g. Cai et al., 2021). The proof of Theorem 4.2 shares some similarity with that of Theorem 4.1, with the Assouad's method replaced by a private version tailored for LDP. While this strategy has been used in the LDP literature for establishing lower bounds for private estimation with independent data (e.g. Duchi et al., 2018; Acharya et al., 2024), our setting is much more involved and thus requires more delicate analysis. In particular, in our lower bound setting, we allow the data (especially price) to be adaptively generated by any pricing policy π , making the data inherently dependent. We provide a sketch of proof below and refer to Section S.7.1 of the supplement for a detailed roadmap of proof and technical contributions. Sketch of proof for Theorem 4.2. The proof is based on the construction of 2 d highly indistinguishable revenue functions, where the details of the construction can be found in Section S.7.2. Given this setup, the remainder of the proof consists of two key components.

- S1. (Lower bounding the regret by classification errors). We first apply a general reduction to relate the regret bound to the classification error in certain classification problems. In particular, inspired by Chen et al. (2022a), we show that for any non-anticipating /epsilon1 -LDP policy π , its regret can be lower bounded by the classification errors of d classification problems subject to 2 /epsilon1 -LDP constraints. This reduction is established in Lemma S.7.4.
- S2. (A new sharp lower bound on classification errors) At a high level, we further lower bound the classification errors in terms of the Hellinger distance between pairs of distributions, with the Hellinger distance itself being upper bounded using the information contraction bound techniques developed in Acharya et al. (2024). Importantly, we make non-trivial modifications to adapt the technical tools in Acharya et al. (2024), which is designed for statistical estimation

problems under LDP with i.i.d. data, to our setting of dynamic pricing under LDP, where the data can be adaptively collected by any non-anticipating pricing policy π and thus exhibit inherent dependencies. To address this, we develop a novel conditioning argument via martingale decomposition when bounding the Hellinger distance between joint distributions, making the intrinsic dependence of the data tractable for analysis. This step is established in Lemma S.7.8.

We remark that our lower bound analysis in Theorem 4.2 is new and first time seen in the dynamic pricing literature (in particular Lemmas S.7.8 and S.7.9). In fact, existing lower bound construction in contextual bandit (Han et al., 2021) and contextual (non-parametric) dynamic pricing (Chen et al., 2022a) under LDP constraints mainly utilize the well-known private Le Cam Lemma and private Assouad's Lemma developed in Duchi et al. (2018), combined with KL divergence bounds. However, we find this strategy can only provide a Ω( √ dT//epsilon1 ) lower bound for our setting, which does not fully characterize the cost of privacy in dimension d. Instead, our proof strategy is to leverage the Hellinger distance, which is sharper than the KL divergence, and adapt the information contraction bound in Acharya et al. (2024) to derive the optimal Ω( d √ T//epsilon1 ) bound. This proof strategy is the first time seen in the literature for online learning under LDP constraints and thus is of independent interest.

## 5 Numerical analysis

In this section, we conduct extensive numerical experiments to investigate the performance of the proposed algorithms and further validate our theoretical findings. Section 5.1 discusses the general simulation settings, followed by synthetic and real-world data analysis in Sections 5.2 and 5.3.

## 5.1 General simulation settings

Data generating process in Section 5.2. We consider a logistic regression setting, where given ( z t , p t ), the consumer demand y t is a Bernoulli random variable such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

with ψ ′ ( · ) being the logistic function. The feature vector { z t = ( z t, 1 , · · · , z t,d ) /latticetop } t are i.i.d. random vectors across time. We consider two simulation scenarios.

(S1) . We set α ∗ = 1 . 6 × 1 d / √ d and β ∗ = 1 d / √ d . For each feature vector z t , we set { z t,i } d i =1 as i.i.d. uniform(1 / √ d, 2 / √ d ) random variables. (S2) . We set α ∗ = 1 d and β ∗ = 1 d . Each feature vector z t is uniformly drawn from { e 1 , . . . , e d } , which consists of the d standard orthonormal basis in R d . For (S1), the optimal price is always in [0 . 91 , 2 . 68] for all d , while for (S2), the optimal price is always 1 . 57. Therefore, we set the price range [ l, u ] to be [0 , 3] for all algorithms in Section 5.2.

Implementation details . For a known horizon T , the algorithms are implemented as follows.

- For ETC, following Theorem 2.2, we set the length of price experiments as τ = ⌈√ dT log( T ) ⌉ .

For the case where T is unknown, all algorithms are implemented with the standard doubling trick.

- For ETC-LDP, following Theorem 3.2, we set the length of price experiments as τ = ⌈ 2 d √ T log( T ) //epsilon1 ⌉ and the learning rate of SGD as ζ l = L p /d . For the L 2 -ball mechanism, it is easy to derive that C g = max t ‖ z t ‖ · √ 1 + u 2 under the logistic regression model in (15). For ETC-LDP-Mixed, following Theorem 3.3, we further set τ ( ̂ p ∗ ) = ⌈ 2 √ dT log( T ) / √ ̂ p ∗ +(1 -̂ p ∗ )( /epsilon1 2 /d ) ⌉ .

Additional simulations in the supplement. To conserve space, numerical experiments on the performance of the proposed UCB algorithm for dynamic pricing under adversarial contexts and the performance of the proposed ETD-LDP-Approx algorithm for dynamic pricing under ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP are presented in Sections S.6.5 and S.4 of the supplement, respectively.

## 5.2 Experiments on synthetic data

## 5.2.1 ETC and supCB with known T

In this section, we examine the performance of ETC and supCB when the horizon T is known. We set the horizon T ∈ { (1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 7) 2 × 10000 } and the dimension d ∈ { (1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5) 2 } = { 1 , 4 , 9 , 16 , 25 } . Given ( T, d ), we conduct 500 independent experiments and record the realized regrets { R ( i ) T,d } 500 i =1 . As discussed in Section 2.2.1, ETC achieves much better statistical and computational efficiency in finite-sample than supCB. We thus refer to Section S.2.1 of the supplement for results of supCB.

Reported metrics : We report the sample mean regret R T,d = ∑ 500 i =1 R ( i ) T,d / 500. Denote S T,d as the sample standard deviation of { R ( i ) T,d } 500 i =1 , we further construct I T,d = [ R T,d -3 S T,d / √ 500 , R T,d +

3 S T,d / √ 500], which is the 99% confidence interval of the expected regret E ( R T,d ) based on normal approximation. Based on { log( R T,d ) } across all ( d, T ), we further fit a linear regression

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

and examine the coefficients ( β d , β T ) . Here, the offset term is used to remove the impact of the polylogarithmic factor that appears in the expected regret E ( R T,d ).

Figure 1 reports the performance of ETC under (S1). Specifically, Figure 1(left) reports the mean regret R T,d (and the confidence interval I T,d ) of ETC at different ( T, d ) under (S1). As can be seen, given a fixed dimension d , the regret scales sublinearly with respect to T , and given a fixed horizon T , the regret increases with the dimension d. Figure 1(middle) plots log( R T,d ) vs. log( T ) for each fixed d , and further plots the estimated regression lines based on (16) with an offset term 0 . 5loglog( T ). In particular, the regression coefficients are estimated as β d = 0 . 48 and β T = 0 . 49, providing some numerical evidence for Theorem 2.2. For illustration, Figure 1(right) further gives the boxplot of { R ( i ) T,d } 500 i =1 for each T ∈ { (1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 7) 2 × 10000 } with a fixed d = 9 . The performance of ETC under (S2) is reported in Figure S.2 of the supplement, where similar phenomenon is observed.

Figure 1: ETC under (S1). [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ). [Middle]: Mean regret (in log scale) with fitted regression lines. [Right]: Boxplot of regrets at different T ( d = 9).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## 5.2.2 ETC-Doubling, MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic with unknown T

In this section, we examine ETC-Doubling and compare with both the original and modified MLECycle and Semi-Myopic algorithms detailed in Sections S.5.4 and S.5.5 of the supplement.

We set T = 49 × 10000 and d ∈ { 1 , 4 , 9 , 16 , 25 } . Note that T is unknown to the pricing algorithm and it only means that we stop at T . Given ( T, d ), for each algorithm, we conduct 500 independent

experiments and record the realized sample paths of regrets { R ( i ) t,d , t ∈ [ T ] } 500 i =1 . For each t ∈ [ T ], we compute its sample mean regret R t,d and confidence interval I t,d as described in Section 5.2.1.

Here, for the q th episode in ETC-Doubling of length T q = 2 q , we set the number of price experiments as τ q = min { T q , ( √ 2 -1) √ dT q log( T q ) } . The √ 2 -1 factor is introduced such that the total price experiments conducted in ETD-Doubling is approximately √ dT log( T ), which matches the number of price experiments conducted by ETC when T is known. We refer to Sections S.5.4 and S.5.5 of the supplement for implementation details of MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic.

Figure 2(left) plots the mean regret path { R t,d , t ∈ [ T ] } and confidence bound { I t,d , t ∈ [ T ] } of ETC-Doubling across d ∈ { 1 , 4 , 9 , 16 , 25 } under (S1). The episodic nature of ETC-Doubling can be clearly seen, as the regret accumulates sharply during the exploration stage of each episode and then switches to a mild increase during exploitation. Comparing Figure 2(left) to Figure 1(left), we can see ETC-Doubling incurs larger regret without the knowledge of the horizon T , which is intuitive.

Figure 2(middle and right) report the performance of modified MLE-Cycle and modified SemiMyopic, which give a smooth regret path due to the design. Figure 3(left) further gives the boxplot of the final regret { R ( i ) T,d } 500 i =1 incurred by ETC-Doubling and modified MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic. Overall, the three achieve similar performances with ETC-Doubling having the smallest regret, especially for large d . Moreover, in Figure 3(middle and right), we compare the boxplot of the final regret { R ( i ) T,d } 500 i =1 of the modified MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic, and their original versions, which underexplore and thus give much higher regret for large d . This further illustrates the importance of our findings. The result under (S2) are reported in Figures S.5 and S.6 of the supplement, where similar findings are observed.

We further report the average computation time of ETC-Doubling, modified MLE-Cycle and SemiMyopic in Table S.1 of the supplement, where it is seen that ETC-Doubling is much more efficient than MLE-Cycle (around 1 . 5 ∼ 6 times faster) and Semi-Myopic (around 1 . 5 ∼ 14 times faster), especially when the dimension d is large, while at the same time ETC-Doubling improves upon MLE-Cycle for around 6-9% and Semi-Myopic for around 20-25% in terms of final regret as suggested in Figure 3(left).

Figure 2: (S1) Mean regret (with C.I.) of ETC-Doubling, modified MLE-Cycle and modified SemiMyopic with unknown T = 49000.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 3: (S1) Boxplot of regrets at T = 490000 for modified MLE-Cycle v.s. modified Semi-Myopic v.s. ETC-Doubling, MLE-Cycle (Original v.s. Modified), and Semi-Myopic (Original v.s. Modified).
