## 5.2.3 ETC-LDP and ETC-LDP-Mixed with known T

In this section, we examine ETC-LDP under /epsilon1 -LDP and ETC-LDP-Mixed under mixed privacy constraints. We set T ∈ { (1 , 3 , 5 , 7 , 9) × 10 5 } and set d ∈ { 1 , 2 , 4 , 6 } . We focus on simulation settings with small dimension d , as estimation and thus pricing are intrinsically much more challenging under the LDP setting. Following the LDP literature (e.g. Duchi et al., 2018), we set /epsilon1 ∈ { 1 , 2 , 4 } , which are the most commonly used LDP parameters in the industry (e.g. Han et al., 2021). For the case of mixed privacy constraints, we further consider the proportion of non-private consumers to be p ∗ ∈ { 0 . 05 , 0 . 1 } .

Recall under LDP, by Assumption 3.1(a), the parameter space Θ is required to be compact. Here, we set Θ = { θ : ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ √ d } , i.e. we allow Θ to grow with the dimension d in the numerical experiments, which is more realistic. For each ( T, d, /epsilon1 ), we conduct 500 independent experiments and

record the realized regrets { R ( i ) T,d,/epsilon1 } 500 i =1 of ETC-LDP. We then compute the sample mean regret R T,d,/epsilon1 and the confidence interval I T,d,/epsilon1 based on { R ( i ) T,d,/epsilon1 } 500 i =1 as described in Section 5.2.1.

Figure 4(left) reports the mean regret R T,d,/epsilon1 of ETC-LDP with /epsilon1 = 1 at different ( T, d ) under (S1). As can be seen, given a fixed d , the regret scales sublinearly with respect to T , and given a fixed horizon T , the regret grows with the dimension d. Compared to ETC in Section 5.2.1, it is clear that to accommodate privacy, ETC-LDP incurs a substantially higher regret (and larger variance). In particular, a careful comparison between Figure 1(left) and Figure 4(left) shows that the mean regret of ETC-LDP is around 7-8 times higher than the mean regret of ETC for comparable ( T, d ) under (S1). This is indeed intuitive as the private SGD estimator is much less efficient than MLE. Figure 4(middle) reports the performance of ETC-LDP with /epsilon1 = 4. Figure 4(right) further gives the boxplot of { R ( i ) T,d,/epsilon1 } 500 i =1 for each T ∈ { (1 , 3 , 5 , 7 , 9) × 10 5 } and /epsilon1 ∈ { 1 , 4 } with a fixed d = 6 . Clearly, as the privacy constraint eases, ETC-LDP achieves lower mean regret with smaller variance.

Figure 4: Performance of ETC-LDP under (S1). [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ) and /epsilon1 = 1. [Middle]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ) and /epsilon1 = 4. [Right]: Boxplot of regrets (based on 500 experiments) at different T (with d = 6) with /epsilon1 ∈ { 1 , 4 } .

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 5(left and middle) report the mean regret R T,d,/epsilon1,p ∗ of ETC-LDP-Mixed with /epsilon1 = 1 and 4 at different ( T, d, p ∗ ). Similar to ETC-LDP, the regret scales sublinearly with T and grows with d. Moreover, a larger proportion of non-private consumers ( p ∗ = 0 . 1) clearly leads to lower regret, especially under small /epsilon1 and large d . Figure 5(right) further compares the boxplot of regret { R ( i ) T,d,/epsilon1 } 500 i =1 achieved by ETC-LDP and ETC-LDP-Mixed (under p ∗ = 0 . 1) for each T ∈ { (1 , 3 , 5 , 7 , 9) × 10 5 } with a fixed d = 6 and /epsilon1 = 1. As can be seen, compared to pure /epsilon1 -LDP, the presence of non-private consumers

can substantially lower regret, confirming the theoretical results in Theorem 3.3. The performance of ETC-LDP and ETC-LDP-Mixed under (S2) is reported in Figures S.7-S.8 of the supplement, where similar phenomenon as the one in Figures 4-5 is observed.

Figure 5: Performance of ETC-LDP-Mixed under (S1). [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ) and /epsilon1 = 1 for p ∗ = (0 . 05 , 0 . 1). [Middle]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ) and /epsilon1 = 4 for p ∗ = (0 . 05 , 0 . 1). [Right]: Boxplot of regrets (based on 500 experiments) at different T (with d = 6 and /epsilon1 = 1) for pure LDP (i.e. p ∗ = 0) and mixed LDP ( p ∗ = 0 . 1).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## 5.3 Real data analysis

In this section, we explore practical utility of the proposed algorithms on a real-world auto loan dataset provided by the Center for Pricing and Revenue Management at Columbia University.

The dataset records all auto loan applications received by a major online lender in the U.S. from July 2002 to November 2004 with a total of 208,085 applications. For each application, we observe information about the loan (e.g. term and amount), the prospective consumer (e.g. FICO score), and the economic environment (e.g. prime rate). We also observe the monthly payment required for the approved loan, which can be viewed as the pricing decision by the company, and whether the offer was accepted - a binary purchasing decision by a consumer.

We follow the feature selection result in Luo et al. (2021) and Bastani et al. (2022) and use the loan amount approved, term, prime rate, the competitor's rate and FICO score as covariates, i.e. d = 5 . We refer to Table S.2 in the supplement for detailed description of the covariates. We further standardize each covariate by its sample mean. The price p of a loan is computed as the net present value of future payment minus the loan amount, i.e. p = Monthly Payment × ∑ Term i =1 (1 + Rate) -i -Loan Amount .

We set Rate as 0 . 12%, an average of the monthly London interbank offered rate for the studied time period. For convenience, we use one thousand dollars ($1000) as the basic unit for loan prices.

Note that it is impossible to obtain consumers' real online responses to any pricing strategy unless they were used in the system when the data were collected. Therefore, following the literature, we first estimate the demand model based on the entire observations and use it as the ground truth to generate consumer responses. In particular, denote the t th consumer decision as y t , the covariates as z t ∈ R 5 , and loan price as p t , we fit the logistic regression model in (15) to { y t , z t , p t } 208085 t =1 . The estimated θ ∗ = ( α ∗ , β ∗ ) is reported in Table S.3 of the supplement. Figure S.9 further gives the histograms of covariates norm {‖ z t ‖ 2 } , price sensitivity { z /latticetop t β ∗ } and optimal prices { p ∗ t } based on the fitted ground truth model. To ensure the optimal price is bounded in [ l, u ] = [0 , 10], we remove covariates z t with highest 1% norm or lowest 1% price sensitivity. We denote the remaining { z t } as Z , which consists of 204782 covariates. We refer to Section S.2.3 for more details of this preprocessing.

We run all algorithms (i.e. ETC-Doubling, modified MLE-Cycle, modified Semi-Myopic, ETCLDP, ETC-LDP-Mixed (assuming 10% consumers are non-private, i.e. p ∗ = 0 . 1) and ETC-LDPApprox (with ∆ = 10 -3 ) based on the ground truth model for T = 208085, where the covariate z t 's are i.i.d. random vectors sampled from Z and y t follows the ground truth model given ( z t , p t ). For each algorithm, we conduct 500 experiments and record regret { R ( i ) T } 500 i =1 . The horizon T is set as unknown for all algorithms. For ETC-Doubling, MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic, the implementation follows that in Section 5.2.2. For ETC-LDP and ETC-LDP-Mixed, the implementation follows that in Section 5.2.3, while to handle the unknown horizon T , we use doubling trick. We refer to Section S.2.3 of the supplement for more implementation details.

Figure 6(left) gives the mean regret path { R t , t ∈ [1 , T ] } and the pointwise confidence bound { I t , t ∈ [1 , T ] } of ETC-Doubling, MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic, where similar phenomenon as that in Figure 2 is seen. In particular, the three methods provide similar performance with ETC-Doubling having a better regret than MLE-Cycle (around 6%) and Semi-Myopic (around 25%), where the final mean regrets of the three algorithms are 2336.43, 2478.64 and 3011.84, respectively. Based on

ep=1

MLE-Cycle

ETC-Doubling ep=2

ep=4

mixed (ep=1)

Semi-Myopic approx (ep=1)

{ log( R t ) } across t ∈ [1 , T ], the estimated linear regression in (16) with an offset term of 0 . 5loglog T gives β T = 0 . 49 for ETC-Doubling, β T = 0 . 48 for MLE-Cycle, and β T = 0 . 46 for Semi-Myopic.

Figure 6(middle) gives the mean regret path { R t , t ∈ [1 , T ] } and the pointwise confidence bound of ETC-LDP for /epsilon1 ∈ { 1 , 2 , 4 } . The final mean regrets are 15591 . 76 , 10057 . 41 and 7021 . 67 for /epsilon1 = 1 , 2 , 4, respectively. It further gives the performance of ETC-LDP-Mixed and ETC-LDP-Approx at /epsilon1 = 1, where the final mean regrets are 12666.85 and 15930.78, respectively. The estimated linear regression in (16) with an offset term of loglog( T ) gives β T = 0 . 57 , 0 . 53 , 0 . 57 for ETC-LDP at /epsilon1 = 1 , 2 , 4, and gives β T = 0 . 53 , 0 . 58 for ETC-LDP-Mixed and ETC-LDP-Approx at /epsilon1 = 1, respectively. For illustration, Figure 6(right) further gives the boxplot of { R ( i ) T } 500 i =1 for all algorithms. Note that compared to non-private dynamic pricing (i.e. ETC-Doubling, MLE-Cycle and Semi-Myopic), ETC-LDP incurs substantially higher regret. With /epsilon1 = 1, ETC-LDP incurs around 6 times higher regret than ETCDoubling. By simple algebra, this translates to 13.26 million dollars loss in revenue for T = 208085 customers, which highlights the trade-off between privacy preservation and profitability of the firm. Moreover, ETC-LDP-Mixed can effectively leverage the non-private consumers (recall p ∗ = 0 . 1) and helps reduce revenue loss of ETC-LDP by around 2.92 million dollars (i.e. 23% improvement).

Figure 6: Performance of different algorithms for real data [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) of ETCDoubling and modified MLE-Cycle and modified Semi-Myopic. [Middle]: Mean regret (with C.I.) of ETC-LDP with /epsilon1 ∈ { 1 , 2 , 4 } and ETC-LDP-Mixed and ETC-LDP-Approx with /epsilon1 = 1. [Right]: Boxplot of regrets of all algorithms (based on 500 experiments) at T = 208085. M. stands for MLE-Cycle, ETC stands for ETC-Doubling and S. stands for Semi-Myopic.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## 6 Conclusion

In this paper, we studied the contextual dynamic pricing problem with a GLM demand model and a stochastic contextual vector of dimension d . For the non-private version, we propose two algorithms, supCB and ETC, that can achieve the optimal regret of order ˜ O ( √ dT ). Importantly, this improves upon the state-of-the-art in the literature by a factor of √ d . In addition, we consider the timely problem of dynamic pricing under LDP and propose a private SGD based ETC-LDP algorithm, which gives a regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T//epsilon1 ), highlighting the cost of privacy. We further establish lower bounds and illustrate the utility of the proposed methods via extensive numerical experiments. One interesting direction for future research is to consider scenarios where there are unknown changes in the context distribution or the parameters of the GLM demand model, which can be more realistic in practice.

## References

- Acharya, J., Canonne, C. L., Sun, Z., and Tyagi, H. (2024). Unified lower bounds for interactive highdimensional estimation under information constraints. Neural Information Processing Systems , 36. Apple (2019). Improving siri's privacy protections. https://apple.co/3GwnLvj .
- Auer, P. (2002). Using confidence bounds for exploitation-exploration trade-offs. Journal of Machine Learning Research , 3(Nov):397-422.
- Auer, P., Cesa-Bianchi, N., Freund, Y., and Schapire, R. E. (1995). Gambling in a rigged casino: The adversarial multi-armed bandit problem. In IEEE FOCS , pages 322-331.
- Ban, G.-Y. and Keskin, N. B. (2021). Personalized dynamic pricing with machine learning: Highdimensional features and heterogeneous elasticity. Management Science , 67(9):5549-5568.
- Bastani, H., Simchi-Levi, D., and Zhu, R. (2022). Meta dynamic pricing: Transfer learning across experiments. Management Science , 68(3):1865-1881.
- Broder, J. and Rusmevichientong, P. (2012). Dynamic pricing under a general parametric choice model. Operations Research , 60(4):965-980.
- Cai, T. T., Wang, Y., and Zhang, L. (2021). The cost of privacy: Optimal rates of convergence for parameter estimation with differential privacy. The Annals of Statistics , 49(5):2825-2850.
- Chen, K., Hu, I., and Ying, Z. (1999). Strong consistency of maximum quasi-likelihood estimators in generalized linear models with fixed and adaptive designs. The Annals of Statistics , 27(4):1155-1163.
- Chen, N. and Gallego, G. (2021). Nonparametric pricing analytics with customer covariates. Operations Research , 69(3):974-984.
- Chen, X., Miao, S., and Wang, Y. (2022a). Differential privacy in personalized pricing with nonparametric demand models. Operations Research .
- Chen, X., Owen, Z., Pixton, C., and Simchi-Levi, D. (2022b). A statistical learning approach to personalization in revenue management. Management Science , 68(3):1923-1937.

- Chen, X., Simchi-Levi, D., and Wang, Y. (2022c). Privacy-preserving dynamic personalized pricing with demand learning. Management Science , 68(7):4878-4898.
- Chu, W., Li, L., Reyzin, L., and Schapire, R. (2011). Contextual bandits with linear payoff functions. In Proceedings of the Fourteenth International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics , pages 208-214. JMLR Workshop and Conference Proceedings.
- Cummings, R., Desfontaines, D., Evans, D., Geambasu, R., Huang, Y., Jagielski, M., Kairouz, P., Kamath, G., Oh, S., Ohrimenko, O., et al. (2024). Advancing differential privacy: Where we are now and future directions for real-world deployment. Harvard Data Science Review , 6(1).
- den Boer, A. V. (2015). Dynamic pricing and learning: historical origins, current research, and new directions. Surveys in operations research and management science , 20(1):1-18.
- Den Boer, A. V. and Zwart, B. (2014). Simultaneously learning and optimizing using controlled variance pricing. Management science , 60(3):770-783.
- Duchi, J. C., Jordan, M. I., and Wainwright, M. J. (2018). Minimax optimal procedures for locally private estimation. Journal of the American Statistical Association , 113(521):182-201.
- Dwork, C., McSherry, F., Nissim, K., and Smith, A. (2006). Calibrating noise to sensitivity in private data analysis. In TCC 2006 , pages 265-284.
- Evfimievski, A., Gehrke, J., and Srikant, R. (2003). Limiting privacy breaches in privacy preserving data mining. In ACM SIGMOD-SIGACT-SIGART , pages 211-222.
- Fan, J., Guo, Y., and Yu, M. (2024). Policy optimization using semiparametric models for dynamic pricing. Journal of the American Statistical Association , 119(545):552-564.
- Google (2014). Learning statistics with privacy, aided by the flip of a coin. https://bit.ly/2RehPPz .
- Han, Y., Liang, Z., Wang, Y., and Zhang, J. (2021). Generalized linear bandits with local differential privacy. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems , 34:26511-26522.
- Javanmard, A. and Nazerzadeh, H. (2019). Dynamic pricing in high-dimensions. Journal of Machine Learning Research , 20(1):315-363.
- Keskin, N. B. and Zeevi, A. (2014). Dynamic pricing with an unknown demand model: Asymptotically optimal semi-myopic policies. Operations Research , 62(5):1142-1167.
- Lattimore, T. and Szepesv´ ari, C. (2020). Bandit algorithms . Cambridge University Press.
- Lei, Y., Miao, S., and Momot, R. (2023). Privacy-preserving personalized revenue management. Management Science .
- Li, L., Lu, Y., and Zhou, D. (2017). Provably optimal algorithms for generalized linear contextual bandits. In International Conference on Machine Learning , pages 2071-2080. PMLR.
- Li, M., Berrett, T. B., and Yu, Y. (2023). On robustness and local differential privacy. The Annals of Statistics , 51(2):717-737.
- Lucas, O., Sokalski, M., and Fisher, R. (2021). Corporate data responsibility: Bridging the consumer trust gap. KPMG Advisory .
- Luo, Y., Sun, W. W., and Liu, Y. (2021). Distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing. arXiv preprint arXiv:2109.07340 .
- Rakhlin, A., Shamir, O., and Sridharan, K. (2011). Making gradient descent optimal for strongly convex stochastic optimization. arXiv preprint arXiv:1109.5647 .
- Simchi-Levi, D. and Wang, C. (2023). Pricing experimental design: Causal effect, expected revenue and tail risk. In International Conference on Machine Learning , pages 31788-31799. PMLR.

Wang, H., Talluri, K., and Li, X. (2024). On dynamic pricing with covariates. Operations Research .

## Supplementary Material - Contextual Dynamic Pricing: Algorithms, Optimality, and Local Differential Privacy Constraints

The supplementary material is organized as follows. Section S.1 provides an in-depth literature review. Section S.2 provides additional results for simulation and real data application. Section S.3 provides details of the supCB algorithm for non-private dynamic pricing. Section S.4 provides details of the UCB algorithm for dynamic pricing under adversarial contexts. Section S.5 provides technical details for theoretical guarantees of the supCB and ETC algorithms in Section 2 of the main text. Section S.6 provides technical details for theoretical guarantees of the ETC-LDP and ETC-LDPMixed algorithm in Section 3 of the main text. An ETC-LDP-Approx algorithm is further proposed and analyzed for dyanmic pricing under ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP. Section S.7 provides technical details for the minimax lower bound results in Section 4 of the main text.

## S.1 Literature review

Three streams of literature are closely related to our paper: dynamic pricing with demand learning, contextual multi-armed bandit, and differential privacy for online learning.

## S.1.1 Dynamic pricing with demand learning

Due to the increasing popularity of online retailing, dynamic pricing with demand learning has been extensively studied across statistics, machine learning, and operations research. Early works in dynamic pricing mainly focus on the context-free setting, see e.g. Besbes and Zeevi (2009) and Broder and Rusmevichientong (2012). In particular, Broder and Rusmevichientong (2012) show that there exist two regimes in context-free dynamic pricing, the well-separated case where demand curves

do not overlap at any price and the general case where there exists an uninformative price. Broder and Rusmevichien (2012) show that the optimal regret for the well-separated case is O (log( T )) and for the general case is O ( √ T ).

For parametric contextual dynamic pricing, Qiang and Bayati (2016) consider the well-separated case with a linear demand function and derive a regret upper bound of order O ( d log( T )), which is further corroborated by Javanmard and Nazerzadeh (2019), with additional results in high-dimensional covariates cases. For the general case, Ban and Keskin (2021) adapt the MLE-cycle algorithm proposed in Broder and Rusmevichientong (2012) and achieve a regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T ) assuming GLM demand functions. Allowing for adversarial contexts, Wang et al. (2024) propose a UCB algorithm and derive the same ˜ O ( d √ T ) rate for the regret under GLM demand functions assumptions.

Beyond parametric models, Chen and Gallego (2021) study contextual dynamic pricing under nonparametric demand models, while Wang et al. (2023), Luo et al. (2024) and Fan et al. (2024) study semiparametric cases. In particular, Fan et al. (2024) show that under a linear valuation model and an infinitely differentiable market noise function, the optimal regret is of order ˜ O ( √ dT ), which in some sense complements our result under the parametric GLM model. Contextual dynamic pricing has also been studied from a wide range of aspects, such as consumer strategic behavior (Liu et al., 2023), non-stationarity (Zhao et al., 2023), fairness constraints (Chen et al., 2023), and differential privacy constraints (see later for more detailed discussion).

## S.1.2 Contextual multi-armed bandit

Most contextual MAB literature focuses on the linear contextual bandit setting where the reward assumes a linear model with r t,a = x /latticetop t,a θ + /epsilon1 t , where x t,a ∈ X is the context associated with arm a , θ is the unknown parameter and /epsilon1 t is the error process. It is well-known that the optimal regret is of order ˜ O ( d √ T ) when the arm space X is a compact (but otherwise arbitrary) subset of R d and can be achieved via UCB (Dani et al., 2008; Abbasi-Yadkori et al., 2011). In contrast, for the setting where the number of available arms K in each round is finite, the regret can be further improved to ˜ O ( √ dT log( K )) (Auer, 2002; Chu et al., 2011). However, such a regret bound can only be achieved

via a more involved confidence bound-type algorithm. This type of algorithm is first proposed by Auer (2002) under the name sup-LinRel and is extended to the generalized linear bandit in Li et al. (2017), which inspires the design of the proposed supCB algorithm in our paper. Note that contextual dynamic pricing can in some sense be viewed as a contextual MAB with infinite number of arms, as the price can take any value in [ l, u ] . However, the key insight of our work is that, despite being uncountable, this action space is of dimension one regardless of the contextual dimensionality d , and thus can be well-approximated by a contextual MAB with reasonably many arms via discretization and achieves an optimal regret of order ˜ O ( √ dT ). S.1.3 Differential privacy for online learning

The most prevalent privacy concept used in both academia and industry is the notion of differential privacy (DP) proposed by Dwork et al. (2006). In the DP literature, there are two widely used privacy schemes known as central differential privacy (CDP, Dwork et al., 2006) and local differential privacy (LDP, Evfimievski et al., 2003).

CDP assumes the existence of a trusted central handler (e.g. the firm) that can securely store and process raw consumer information. This may raise concerns about data vulnerability, particularly the risk of attacks on the central server, leading to unauthorized access to sensitive customer information. In contrast, LDP offers a decentralized approach that prioritizes individual privacy. Instead of relying on a central authority to store and process data, LDP allows each customer to locally randomize their data before sharing them with the firm or any other party.

Due to the practical importance of privacy, there is a recent surge in designing online learning algorithms with DP guarantees for contextual MAB and contextual dynamic pricing. Shariff and Sheffet (2018) study linear contextual bandits under CDP, while Zheng et al. (2020) explore them under LDP. Based on a covariate-diversity assumption, Han et al. (2021) propose an exploration-free algorithm for generalized linear bandits under LDP. However, this assumption in general does not hold under dynamic pricing as the adaptively chosen price is part of the covariate.

Chen et al. (2022b) examine dynamic pricing under a GLM demand model with CDP constraints

and Chen et al. (2022a) study nonparametric dynamic pricing under both LDP and CDP constraints. Under the offline setting, where all historical data are accessible for learning, Lei et al. (2023) study contextual dynamic pricing for a linear demand model under LDP, focusing primarily on preserving the privacy of contexts. Leveraging the structure of the linear model, they propose a private ordinary least squares estimator, which cannot be used for GLM. In this paper, we consider contextual dynamic pricing with a GLM demand function under the online setting and propose a stochastic gradient descent based algorithm that offers LDP guarantees for both consumer responses and contexts.

## S.2 Additional numerical results

## S.2.1 Additional results for ETC and supCB with known T

This section provides additional numerical results for the regret and computational time of ETC and supCB with known T as in Section 5.2.1 of the main text.

Implementation details of supCB . Following Theorem S.3.1, we set the number of stages as S = /floorleft log 2 ( T ) /floorright , the length of price experiments as τ = ⌈ √ dT ⌉ and the discretization rate as K = ⌈√ T/d/ log( T ) ⌉ . We set α = loglog( T ) · √ log(3 T 1 . 5 KS ). Here, a loglog( T ) factor is included in α as in practice ( σ, M ψ 2 , κ ) is unknown. This at most inflates the regret of supCB by loglog( T ) .

Results . Figure S.1 reports the performance of supCB under (S1). The estimated linear regression based on (16) with an offset term of 1 . 5loglog( T ) gives ( β d , β T ) = (0 . 50 , 0 . 49), which provides numerical evidence for Theorem S.3.1. However, though its regret scales sublinearly with ( d, T ), supCB records an expected regret that is overall 3 to 4 times higher than ETC (see Figure 1 of the main text). The performance of ETC and supCB under (S2) is reported in Figure S.2 and Figure S.3, where similar phenomenon is observed. Therefore, in terms of statistical efficiency, ETC is preferred over supCB.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure S.1: supCB under (S1). [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ). [Middle]: Mean regret (in log scale) with fitted regression lines. [Right]: Boxplot of regrets at different T ( d = 9).

Figure S.2: Performance of ETC under (S2) [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ). [Middle]: Mean regret (in log scale) with fitted linear regression lines (( β d , β T ) = (0 . 51 , 0 . 45)). [Right]: Boxplot of regrets (based on 500 experiments) at different T (with d = 9).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure S.3: Performance of supCB under (S2) [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ). [Middle]: Mean regret (in log scale) with fitted linear regression lines (( β d , β T ) = (0 . 47 , 0 . 51)). [Right]: Boxplot of regrets (based on 500 experiments) at different T (with d = 9).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Computation time . Figure S.4 reports the mean computation time of ETC and supCB at different

( T, d ) computed over 500 experiments under (S1). Overall, conducting ETC is faster than supCB by around 10 ∼ 100 times (with an average of 54 times), where the improvement is larger under larger dimension d. Therefore, to summarize, in practice, we recommend to use ETC as the default algorithm for dynamic pricing, as it is simple and achieves better statistical and computational efficiency.

Figure S.4: Average computation time (with C.I.) of ETC and supCB under (S1).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

|--------|--------------|-----------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| α      | - 1 . 434    | 0.288     | - 2 . 348 |        0.807 |  2.845 |

Data preprocessing . Figure S.9(left and middle) gives the histograms of covariates norm {‖ z t ‖ 2 } 208085 t =1 and price sensitivity { z /latticetop t β ∗ } 208085 t =1 computed via the fitted ground truth model. The 99% quantile of covariates norm (which is 3.05) and 1% quantile of price sensitivity (which is 0.13) are further marked via red vertical lines. To impose an upper bound on the covariates norm and a lower bound on the price sensitivity, we remove covariates z t with norm larger than 3.05 or price sensitivity smaller than 0.13. We denote the remaining { z t } as Z , which consists of 204782 covariates. Figure S.9(right) further gives the histogram of the optimal prices (in thousands) of all covariates in Z based on the

ground truth model, where the maximum is 9.994 and the minimum is 0.632 (in thousands).

Figure S.9: Histograms of covariate norm, price sensitivity and optimal price.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Implementation details . For ETC-LDP, the implementation follows that in Section 5.2.3, while to handle the unknown horizon T , we use doubling trick. In particular, for the q th episode, which is of length T q = 2 q , we set the number of price experiments as τ q = min { T q , d √ 2 T q log T q //epsilon1 } , where recall /epsilon1 is the privacy parameter. To be more realistic, we set Θ = { θ : ‖ θ -θ ◦ ‖ ≤ √ d } , where for each experiment, θ o is uniformly sampled from the unit ball centered at θ ∗ . In other words, θ o is a perturbed version of θ ∗ . Since ETC-LDP requires more price experiments, we set the price range [ l, u ] to be [0 , 3] for price experiments and keep the price range to be [0 , 10] for price exploitation, which improves its performance.

## S.3 The supCB algorithm

As discussed in the main text, a key element of supCB is to approximate the contextual dynamic pricing problem via contextual MAB based on discretization. In particular, we approximate the price interval [ l, u ], which is a one-dimensional continuous action space, by a set of K equispaced price points P K = { p ( a ) } a ∈ [ K ] , where p ( a ) = l + ( a -1)( u -l ) / ( K -1) for a ∈ [ K ]. We thus have a generalized linear bandit (GLB) with K arms, where x t,a = ( z /latticetop t , -p ( a ) · z /latticetop t ) /latticetop is the context associated with arm a ∈ [ K ] at time t. We name it revenue -GLB. Unlike the standard GLB (e.g. Filippi et al., 2010; Li et al., 2017), where the reward is defined as the conditional mean E ( y t | x t,a ) = ψ ′ ( x /latticetop t,a θ ∗ ), our reward here is the more complicated revenue function r ( p ( a ) , z t , θ ∗ ) = p ( a ) · ψ ′ ( x /latticetop t,a θ ∗ ) . The optimal

arm at time t is therefore the arm associated with p o t ∈ arg max p ∈P K r ( p, z t , θ ∗ ).

Denote π as a policy for the revenue-GLB based on P K . By simple algebra, its regret for the original contextual dynamic pricing problem as defined in (4) can be rewritten as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Importantly, Lemma S.5.2 in the supplement shows that the class of revenue functions { r ( p, z, θ ∗ ) : z ∈ Z} is uniformly L r -Lipschitz continuous with L r := M ψ 1 + uu b M ψ 2 . This implies that the approximation error is of order O ( T/K ) and thus can be easily controlled.

Therefore, the key to an optimal regret R π T is an algorithm that can efficiently bound the regret of the revenue-GLB. We then propose supCB in Algorithm S.2, with a CB-GLM subroutine in Algorithm S.1. Similar to standard UCB algorithms (e.g. Lai and Robbins, 1985), the idea of supCB is to balance exploration and exploitation via confidence bounds. However, to achieve the near-optimal ˜ O ( √ dT ) regret, supCB is substantially more involved than the standard UCB in its algorithm design.

We first discuss the CB-GLM subroutine. In particular, based on an observed sample { y t , x t } t ∈ Ψ , CB-GLM first estimates θ ∗ via the MLE ̂ θ and constructs the design matrix V = ∑ t ∈ Ψ x t x /latticetop t . It then provides an estimator r a = p ( a ) · ψ ′ ( x /latticetop a ̂ θ ) and a confidence bound w a = α ‖ x a ‖ V -1 , for the expected revenue r ( p ( a ) , z, θ ∗ ) = p ( a ) · ψ ′ ( x /latticetop a θ ∗ ) of arm x a = ( z /latticetop , -p ( a ) z /latticetop ) /latticetop at a new context z . Note that given Theorem 2.1(iii), by the smoothness of ψ ′ ( · ) and boundedness of p ( a ) , we have that w a can serve as a valid high probability confidence bound for | r a -r ( p ( a ) , z, θ ∗ ) | with an appropriately chosen α .

## Algorithm S.1 The CB-GLM subroutine

Input : A new context vector z ∈ R d , price candidate index set A ⊂ [ K ], and data index set Ψ ⊂ [ T ], confidence level parameter α &gt; 0.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

c. For all a ∈ A , set x a = ( z /latticetop , -p ( a ) z /latticetop ) /latticetop , r a = p ( a ) · ψ ′ ( x /latticetop a ̂ θ ) and w a = α ‖ x a ‖ V -1 . d. Return { r a , w a } a ∈ A .

However, Theorem 2.1 requires the crucial assumption that { y t } t ∈ Ψ are conditionally independent given { x t } t ∈ Ψ . Such an assumption in general does not hold when the sample { y t , x t } t ∈ Ψ are adaptively

generated based on standard bandit algorithms such as UCB, as the outcome of y t will influence the future arm choice x t ′ for t ′ &gt; t. This issue is solved via a decomposition technique developed in Auer (2002). This technique is previously used in linear contextual bandits (e.g. Auer, 2002; Chu et al., 2011) and GLB (e.g. Li et al., 2017) to create conditionally independent samples.

In supCB (Algorithm S.2), we adapt this technique to the revenue-GLB. In particular, in each round t , supCB decomposes its decision process into S = /floorleft log 2 ( T ) /floorright stages. At stage s ∈ [ S ], in step I, we first call CB-GLM (based on samples in F s ∪ Ψ s ) to compute the estimated revenue r ( s ) t,a and confidence bound w ( s ) t,a for all remaining candidate arms. In step II, if w ( s ) t,a &gt; 2 -s for some a (i.e. uncertainty is high), supCB will choose arm a for exploration, collect the result in Ψ s and move to round t +1. Otherwise, step III checks whether all w ( s ) t,a ≤ 1 / √ T and if satisfied, we simply pick the arm with highest r ( s ) t,a and move to round t +1 as no exploration is needed. If not, the arms are filtered in step IV to ensure the price points passed to the next stage s +1 are close enough to the optimal one. Since all w ( s ) t,a ≤ 2 -s in step IV and w ( s ) t,a is a high probability bound on | r ( s ) t,a -r ( p ( a ) , z t , θ ∗ ) | , we know that an arm a cannot be optimal if r ( s ) t,a &lt; max j ∈ A s r ( s ) t,j -2 · 2 -s . Note that this decision process is guaranteed to terminate in at most S stages since 1 / √ T &gt; 2 -S by design.

Denote Ψ s ( t ) as the sample collected in stage s before round t and denote Ψ o s ( t ) = Ψ s ( t ) ∪F s . The key property of supCB is that due to the decomposition design, whether an arm x t,a is selected in stage s depends on historical data ∪ s ′ &lt;s { y i , x i } i ∈ Ψ o s ′ ( t ) in previous stages and the confidence bound w ( s ) t,a , which only depends on historical covariates { x i } i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) in stage s . Importantly, the arm choice does not depend on past outcomes { y i } i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) in stage s. Intuitively, this provides the desired conditional independence of { y i } i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) given { x i } i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) and we refer to Lemma S.5.3 for the formal proof.

Note that supCB starts with a pure exploration phase during which p t is drawn uniformly from P K . Denote µ p and σ 2 p as the mean and variance of the uniform distribution on P K and denote Σ p = [1 , -µ p ; -µ p , µ 2 p + σ 2 p ] ∈ R 2 × 2 . For all K ∈ N + , it is easy to show that µ p = ( u + l ) / 2, σ 2 p ≥ ( u -l ) 2 / 12 and that Σ p is a positive definite matrix with

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## Algorithm S.2 The supCB algorithm for dynamic pricing

Input : Total rounds T , price interval [ l, u ], discretization rate K , exploration length τ , confidence parameter α .

Initialization : Discretize the price interval [ l, u ] into K equispaced points as in P K = { p ( a ) } a ∈ [ K ] . Set the number of stages S = /floorleft log 2 T /floorright and set Ψ 0 = Ψ 1 = · · · = Ψ S = ∅ .

The pure exploration phase creates an i.i.d. sample of size τ (collected into F s ) for each stage s ∈ [ S ], which together with (6) ensures that the minimum eigenvalue of the design matrix based on Ψ o s ( t ) = Ψ s ( t ) ∪ F s surpasses the threshold required in Theorem 2.1(iii). Theorem S.3.1 establishes both high-probability and expectation upper bounds on the regret of supCB when T is known.

Theorem S.3.1. Suppose Assumption 2.1 holds. For any δ ∈ (0 , 1) , set K = √ T/d/ log( T ), τ = √ dT and α = 3 σuM ψ 2 /κ · √ log(3 TKS/δ ). Recall S = /floorleft log 2 ( T ) /floorright . Provided that we have that, with probability at least 1 -δ -2log( T ) /T , the regret of the supCB algorithm in Algorithm S.2 is upper bounded by R T ≤ B S 3 · √ dT log( T ) log( T/δ ) log( T/d ), where B S 1 , B S 2 , B S 3 &gt; 0 are absolute constants that only depend on quantities in Assumption 2.1. In particular, setting δ = 1 / √ T , we have that E ( R T ) /lessorsimilar log 3 / 2 ( T ) √ dT.

The details of B S 1 , B S 2 , B S 3 can be found in the proof of Theorem S.3.1 in the supplement.

Theorem S.3.1 states that when the sample size T is sufficiently large, supCB can achieve a regret of order O ( √ dT log 3 / 2 ( T )), which is optimal up to logarithmic terms (see the lower bound Ω( √ dT ) in Theorem 4.1 later). To our knowledge, this improves the best available regret in the contextual dynamic pricing literature (e.g. Ban and Keskin, 2021; Wang et al., 2024) by a factor of √ d .

As discussed in the introduction, there are two types of contextual MAB problems. For the first type, its arm space X is a d -dimensional (uncountable) compact subset of R d and the optimal regret ˜ O ( d √ T ) can be achieved via standard UCB (Dani et al., 2008; Abbasi-Yadkori et al., 2011). The second type has a finite K number of arms in each round t , where the optimal regret is ˜ O ( √ dT log( K )) (Auer, 2002; Chu et al., 2011). The key insight of Theorem S.3.1 and supCB is that the contextual dynamic pricing problem can be essentially viewed as a contextual MAB of the second type. The intuition is that, despite being uncountable, the action space [ l, u ] of dynamic pricing is of dimension one regardless of the context dimension d . Moreover, thanks to the uniform Lipschitz continuity of the revenue function, we can discretize the action space [ l, u ] into K arms and achieve a controllable approximation error as long as K is of polynomial order in T (i.e. O ( √ T ), independent of d ).

Unknown T . When T is unknown, we run supCB with the standard doubling trick (e.g. Auer et al., 1995) in the bandit literature. In particular, we partition N + into non-overlapping episodes, with the k th episode of length E k = 2 k . We run Algorithm S.2 with T = E k for the k th episode and then restart at the next episode. To conserve space, we refer to Theorem S.5.1 for its theoretical guarantee. See also Algorithm 2 later for a detailed implementation of ETC with the doubling trick.

## S.3.1 A modified supCB for adversarial contexts

In the following, we propose a (slightly) modified supCB algorithm based on regularized MLE for adversarial contexts. It requires a stronger assumption on the convexity of the link function ψ ( · ) as is documented in Assumption S.4.1(b). In particular, a linear demand model satisfies this assumption as ψ ( x ) = 1 / 2 x 2 in such case.

Assumption S.3.1. (a) The feature vector { z t } t ∈ [ T ] ⊂ Z ⊆ { z ∈ R d : ‖ z ‖ ≤ 1 } is an arbitrary

sequence of bounded vectors. (b) The function ψ ( · ) is three times continuously differentiable and its second order derivative ψ ′′ ( a ) ≥ c ψ &gt; 0 for all a ∈ R , where c ψ ≤ 1 is an absolute constant. (c) The true model parameter is bounded with ‖ θ ∗ ‖ ≤ c θ for some absolute constant c θ .

Non-asymptotic bounds for regularized MLE : For τ ∈ N + , given a sample { ( y t , x t ) } t ∈ [ τ ] , the log-likelihood of the GLM takes the form L ( θ ) = ∑ τ t =1 { y t x /latticetop t θ -ψ ( x /latticetop t θ ) } . We define the regularized (negative) log-likelihood function

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Lemma S.3.1 establishes a non-asymptotic bounds for the regularized MLE ˜ θ τ with Assumption S.4.1. It can be viewed as the counterpart of Theorem 2.1(iii) under the adversarial contexts.

Note that ˜ L ( θ ) is strongly convex due to the ‖ θ ‖ 2 2 term and thus always has a unique global minimizer ˜ θ τ over R 2 d for any sample { ( y t , x t ) } t ∈ [ τ ] . Denote ˜ V τ = ∑ τ t =1 x t x /latticetop t + I 2 d as the regularized design matrix.

Lemma S.3.1. Suppose that Assumption 2.1(c)-(d) and Assumption S.4.1 hold and { y t } t ∈ [ τ ] are conditionally independent given { x t } t ∈ [ τ ] such that f ( y 1 , · · · , y τ | x 1 , · · · , x τ ) = ∏ τ t =1 f ( y t | x t ; θ ∗ ), where f ( y t | x t ; θ ∗ ) is the true GLM model in (1). We have that for any τ ≥ 0, any δ ∈ (0 , 1) and any x ∈ R 2 d , with probability at least 1 -δ , it holds that

A modified supCB algorithm : Based on Lemma S.3.1, we modify the CB-GLM subroutine in Algorithm S.1 into a regularized version. In particular, instead of using the log-likelihood L ( θ ) and MLE ̂ θ , we use the regularized log-likelihood ˜ L ( θ ) and MLE ˜ θ in step a of CB-GLM and further modify the design matrix V to its regularized version ˜ V = ∑ i ∈ Ψ x i x /latticetop i + I 2 d in step b of CB-GLM. Moreover, due to the use of regularized log-likelihood, we no longer require the exploration stage in the original supCB algorithm and thus set τ ≡ 0 in Algorithm S.2.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Theorem S.3.2 gives theoretical guarantees for the modified supCB algorithm under adversarial contexts. In particular, it shows that the modified supCB algorithm can achieve the near-optimal regret of order ˜ O ( √ dT ) under the adversarial contexts with the additional Assumption S.4.1.

Theorem S.3.2. Suppose Assumption 2.1 holds. For any δ ∈ (0 , 1) , set K = √ T/d/ log( T ), τ = 0 and α = 3 σuM ψ 2 /c ψ · √ log(3 TKS/δ ). Recall S = /floorleft log 2 ( T ) /floorright . For any T , we have that, with probability at least 1 -δ -2log( T ) /T , the regret of the modified supCB algorithm is upper bounded by R T ≤ B S 3 · √ dT log( T ) log( T/δ ) log( T/d ), where B S 1 , B S 2 , B S 3 &gt; 0 are absolute constants that only depend on quantities in Assumption 2.1 and Assumption S.4.1. In particular, setting δ = 1 / √ T , we have that E ( R T ) /lessorsimilar log 3 / 2 ( T ) √ dT.

The proof of Theorem S.3.2 follows the same structure as that of Theorem S.3.1. In particular, the results in Lemmas S.5.2, S.5.3 and S.5.5 continue to hold for the modified supCB algorithm. The only modification is to re-establish the high probability confidence bound results in Lemma S.5.4 (in particular, (S.13)) based on the newly developed Lemma S.3.1 (previously established based on Theorem 2.1(c)), which is straightforward. The detailed proof is thus omitted to conserve space.

## S.3.2 Technical details

Proof of Lemma S.3.1 . In the proof, we drop the subscript τ in θ for notational simplicity. By the strong convexity and differentiability of ˜ L ( θ ), we have that ˜ L ′ ( ˜ θ ) = 0, where recall ˜ θ is the unique global minimizer of ˜ L ( θ ) over R 2 d .

In particular, for any θ ∈ R 2 d , by Taylor expansion, we have that

By Cauchy-Schwartz inequality and the fact that ˜ L ′′ ( θ + ) = ∑ τ t =1 ψ ′′ ( x /latticetop t θ + ) x t x /latticetop t + I 2 d ≥ I 2 d , the above equation holds if ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ &gt; 2 ‖ ˜ L ′ ( θ ∗ ) ‖ = 2 ‖ ∑ τ t =1 ( y t -ψ ′ ( x /latticetop t θ ∗ ) x t ‖ = 2 ‖ ∑ τ t =1 ε t x t ‖ . In other words, the unique minimizer ˜ θ of ˜ L ( θ ) over R 2 d can always be found in the domain { θ : ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ ‖ ∑ τ t =1 ε t x t ‖ } and we have that L ′ ( θ ) = 0.

˜ ˜ Furthermore, by a Taylor expansion of ˜ L ′ ( ˜ θ ), we have that 0 = ˜ L ′ ( ˜ θ ) = ˜ L ′ ( θ ∗ ) + ˜ L ′′ ( θ + )( ˜ θ -θ ∗ ),

where recall ˜ V τ = ∑ τ t =1 x t x /latticetop t + I 2 d . Therefore, we have that

P { | x /latticetop ˜ Σ -1 τ S τ | &gt; c } ≤ 2exp { -c 2 2 ∑ τ t =1 ( x /latticetop ˜ Σ -1 τ x t ) 2 σ 2 } ≤ 2exp    -c 2 2 σ 2 c -2 ψ ‖ x ‖ 2 ˜ V -1 τ    , where the second inequality follows from that

## S.4 Dynamic pricing under adversarial contexts

In this section, we further study dynamic pricing under adversarial contexts. In particular, we relax the stochastic contexts condition in Assumption 2.1(a)-(b) of the main text to Assumption S.4.1, where we only assume { z t } is a sequence of bounded vectors.

Assumption S.4.1. (a) The feature vector { z t } t ∈ [ T ] ⊂ Z ⊆ { z ∈ R d : ‖ z ‖ ≤ 1 } is an arbitrary sequence of bounded vectors. (b). The parameter space Θ is compact such that ‖ Θ ‖ ≤ c θ .

Due to the adversarial nature of { z t } , the design matrix of the log-likelihood function L ( θ ) can be rank deficient. Therefore, for model estimation, we instead rely on the regularized negative loglikelihood function where λ &gt; 0 is a constant (e.g. λ = 1).

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Algorithm S.3 proposes a novelly designed UCB algorithm for dynamic pricing under the adversarial contexts. We further establish its theoretical guarantees in Theorem S.4.1, where we show that it can achieve a regret upper bound of order ˜ O ( d √ T ) under adversarial contexts, matching prior works in the literature (Wang et al., 2024).

## Algorithm S.3 The UCB algorithm for dynamic pricing

Input : Tuning parameter λ , price interval [ l, u ], confidence parameter α , parameter space Θ. Initialization : Set V 0 = λI 2 d .

for t = 1 , 2 , · · · , T do

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

a. Solve the regularized regression and obtain ̂ θ t = arg min θ ∈ Θ ˜ L t -1 ( θ ). b. Solve the one-dimensional optimization c. Offer price p t , record y t and x t = ( z /latticetop t , -p t z /latticetop t ) /latticetop . Set V t = V t -1 + x t x /latticetop t .

where we denote x ( p ) t = ( z /latticetop t , -pz /latticetop t ) /latticetop . end for

Theorem S.4.1. Suppose Assumption 2.1(c)-(e) and Assumption S.4.1 hold. Set λ = 1 and α = B A 1 √ d log( T (1 + u 2 ) /δ ). We have for any T &gt; 0, the regret of UCB is upper bounded by R T ≤ B A 2 · d √ T log( T/δ ), where B A 1 , B A 2 &gt; 0 are absolute constants that only depend on quantities in Assumption 2.1(c)-(e) and Assumption S.4.1.

The key novelty of our proposed UCB algorithm lies in its design of the upper confidence bound for the revenue function (i.e. (S.5)), which takes a much simpler structure than existing UCB algorithms in prior works (Wang et al. (2024), see below for more discussions) and thus is computationally more

efficient. In particular, simple algebra shows that the upper confidence bound U ( p, z t ) in (S.5) takes the form U ( p, z t ) = p · ψ ′ ( a t 0 + a t 1 p + a t 2 p 2 ) , for some a t 0 , a t 1 , a t 2 , which is a one-dimensional function of p and can therefore be optimized efficiently.

Intuition of our UCB : We now give some intuition of our UCB U ( p, z t ) in (S.5). Recall that the true revenue function takes the form r ( p, z t , θ ∗ ) = p · ψ ′ ( x ( p ) /latticetop t θ ∗ ), where we denote x ( p ) t = ( z /latticetop t , -pz /latticetop t ) /latticetop . A key observation is that due to the structure of r ( p, z t , θ ∗ ) and the fact that p &gt; 0, an upper bound of ψ ′ ( x ( p ) /latticetop t θ ∗ ) directly leads to a upper bound of r ( p, z t , θ ∗ ). Moreover, due to the intrinsic convexity of GLM (i.e. ψ ′′ ( · ) &gt; 0, see Assumption 2.1(e)), we have ψ ′ ( · ) is a strictly increasing function and thus an upper bound of x ( p ) /latticetop t θ ∗ directly leas to an upper bound of ψ ′ ( x ( p ) /latticetop t θ ∗ ). This argument leads to our upper confidence bound U ( p, z t ), as we show that, with a properly chosen parameter α , it holds that x ( p ) /latticetop t ̂ θ t + α ‖ x ( p ) t ‖ V -1 t -1 is a high probability upper bound for x ( p ) /latticetop t θ ∗ for all p ∈ [ l, u ] and t ∈ [ T ].

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Comparison with existing works : In the literature, Wang et al. (2024) is the only work that considers dynamic pricing under adversarial contexts with GLM demand models. In particular, Wang et al. (2024) proposes a (computationally difficult) UCB algorithm and shows its regret upper bound is ˜ O ( d √ T ) . Their UCB takes the form (in our notation)

where C t = { θ ∈ Θ : ‖ θ -̂ θ t ‖ V t -1 ≤ α } is the confidence set for θ at time t . As noted by Wang et al. (2024), the inner optimization of ˜ U ( p, z t ) is a 2 d -dimensional non-convex optimization and is difficult to solve. To bypass the computational difficulty, the authors propose to approximate the optimization in (S.6) via a heuristic Monte Carlo approach, which then incurs a worse regret upper bound than ˜ O ( d √ T ) and thus is unsatisfactory.

An an alternative, Wang et al. (2024) further proposes a Thompson Sampling based algorithm that is computationally feasible and achieves the ˜ O ( d √ T ) regret upper bound. However, it requires a strong assumption (see Assumption 4 therein) that there exists an absolute constant g &gt; 0 such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where ˜ Θ = { θ ∈ R 2 d : ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ 2 ≤ c · √ d log T } for some constant c &gt; 0 . Note this may not hold

even for logistic regression since ˜ Θ is not compact but expands with d, T . In addition, our numerical experiments in the next section show that our UCB algorithm is computationally more efficient than the TS algorithm in Wang et al. (2024).

## S.4.1 Numerical experiments

In this section, we conduct numerical experiments to compare the finite sample performance and computational time of our proposed UCB algorithm in Algorithm S.3 and the Thompson Sampling based algorithm (hereafter TS-WTL) in Wang et al. (2024). Note that we choose not to implement the UCB algorithm in Wang et al. (2024) since it requires a 2 d -dimensional non-convex optimization and thus is computationally highly demanding. Moreover, the numerical result in Wang et al. (2024) indicates that their heuristic Monte Carlo based UCB algorithm has very similar performance in terms of regret as their Thompson Sampling based algorithm.

For the demand model, we follow Section 5.1 of the main text and consider the logistic regression setting as in (15) with simulation scenario (S1), where α ∗ = 1 . 6 · 1 d / √ d and β ∗ = 1 d / √ d . However, the feature vector { z t = ( z t, 1 , · · · , z t,d ) /latticetop } t are not i.i.d. but adversarial. In particular, we follow the setting in Wang et al. (2024) and consider two scenarios.

(A1) . For t ≤ T/ 2, we set z t = ( z ∗ t , 1 . 5 · 1 d/ 2 ) / √ d , and for t &gt; T/ 2, we set z t = (1 . 5 · 1 d/ 2 , z ∗ t ) / √ d , where z is a d/ 2-dimensional random vector with z d/ 2 being i.i.d. uniform(0 , 3) random variables.

(A2) . For all t ∈ [ T ], we set z t = ( z t , 1 . 5 · 1 d/ 2 ) / d , where z t is a d/ 2-dimensional random vector with { z ∗ t,i } d/ 2 i =1 being i.i.d. uniform(0 , 3) random variables.

∗ t { ∗ t,i } i =1 ∗ √ ∗

By design, the feature vector { z t } is adversarial under both (A1) and (A2), where its covariance matrix is always of rank d/ 2 at any time t ∈ [ T ], making accurate estimation of the model parameter infeasible. We set the confidence parameter as α = √ d log T log(log T ) / 10 for the UCB algorithm in Algorithm S.3 and follow Wang et al. (2024) for the implementation of the TS-WTL algorithm. Similar to Wang et al. (2024), we set the horizon T ∈ { 1000 , 2500 , 5000 , 7500 , 10000 } and set the dimension d ∈ { 4 , 8 , 12 , 16 } .

Figure S.10 reports the performance of UCB and TS-WTL under (A1). Specifically, Figure S.10(left)

reports the mean regret R T,d (and the confidence interval I T,d ) of TS-WTL and UCB at different ( T, d ) under (A1). As can be seen, despite the adversarial nature of the feature vectors, for both UCB and TS-WTL, given a fixed dimension d , the regret scales sublinearly with respect to T , providing numerical evidence for Theorem S.4.1. In addition, given a fixed horizon T , the regret of both algorithms increase with the dimension d . Moreover, UCB consistently achieves lower regret than TS-WTL. For illustration, Figure S.10(middle) further gives the boxplot of { R ( i ) T,d } 500 i =1 for both UCB and TS-WTL at each d ∈ { 4 , 8 , 12 , 16 } with a fixed T = 10000 . Figure S.10(right) reports the average computational time (and the confidence interval) of TS-WTL and UCB at different ( T, d ) under (A1), where UCB is seen to be computationally more efficient than TS-WTL. The performance of UCB and TS-WTL under (A2) is reported in Figure S.11 of the supplement, where similar phenomenon is observed.

Figure S.10: Performance of TS-WTL and UCB under (A1). [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ). [Middle]: Boxplot of regrets at different d (with T = 10000). [Right]: Mean comp. time (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure S.11: Performance of TS-WTL and UCB under (A2). [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ). [Middle]: Boxplot of regrets at different d (with T = 10000). [Right]: Mean comp. time (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## S.4.2 Proof of Theorem S.4.1

Proof of Theorem S.4.1 . Fix any time point t, denote p ∗ t as the optimal price given z t .

First, by Lemma S.4.1, with probability at least 1 -δ , we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for all p ∈ [ l, u ] and t ∈ [ T ] .

Define M o ψ 2 := sup x ∈X ,θ ∈ Θ | ψ ′′ ( x /latticetop θ ) | ∨ 1, which is an absolute constant. We have that the regret on time t can be bounded via

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the first inequality follows from (S.7) and that ψ ′ ( · ) is an increasing function, the second inequality follows from the definition of p t , the third inequality follows from a Taylor expansion and the last inequality follows from (S.7) and Cauchy-Schwartz. Note that one can clip the upper confidence

bound x ( p ) /latticetop t ̂ θ t + α ‖ x ( p ) t ‖ V -1 t -1 at sup x ∈X ,θ ∈ Θ x /latticetop θ for all p and t, which ensures M o ψ 2 is sufficient for the third inequality. We omit this for notational simplicity.

Therefore, we have that with probability at least 1 -δ , the total regret can be bounded via where the second inequality follows from Cauchy-Schwartz, and the third and fourth inequalities follows from Lemmas 11 and 10 in Abbasi-Yadkori et al. (2011) respectively.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Lemma S.4.1. Denote ∆ t = ̂ θ t -θ ∗ . For any δ &gt; 0, with probability at least 1 -δ , for all t &gt; 0, we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where B A 1 is an absolute constant that only depend on λ and quantities in Assumption 2.1(c)-(e) and Assumption S.4.1.

Proof. By the definition of ̂ θ t , we have ˜ L ( ̂ θ t ) ≤ ˜ L ( θ ∗ ) . Define L t ( θ ) = ∑ t i =1 y i x /latticetop i θ -ψ ( x /latticetop i θ ) as the log-likelihood. By simple algebra and Taylor expansion, we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where κ o := inf x ∈X ,θ ∈ Θ ψ ′′ ( x /latticetop θ ) ∧ 1 &gt; 0 is an absolute constant.

Rearranging the above equation and use the fact that ‖ Θ ‖ &lt; c θ , we further have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

By the self-normalized bound for vector-valued martingales as in Theorem 1 in Abbasi-Yadkori et al. (2011), we have that with probability at least 1 -δ , for all t &gt; 0, it holds that where the second inequality follows from Lemma 10 in Abbasi-Yadkori et al. (2011) and the fact that ‖ x t ‖ 2 ≤ √ 1 + u 2 for all t . Therefore, we have with probability at least 1 -δ , it holds for all t that

which provides the confidence set for θ ∗ .

## S.5 Technical details in Section 2 and Section S.3

The results collected in the Appendix rely on notation defined in (2) of the main text. For convenience, we copy them here:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## S.5.1 Key technical results

Proposition S.5.1 (Matrix Bernstein Inequality) . For τ ∈ N + , define V τ = ∑ τ t =1 x t x /latticetop t , where { x t ∈ R d } t ∈ [ τ ] are i.i.d. from an unknown distribution with ‖ x 1 ‖ ≤ u x , u x &gt; 0. Denote Σ x = E ( x 1 x /latticetop 1 ). We have for all ∆ ≥ 0,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Proof of Proposition S.5.1 . We prove by verifying the condition of the matrix Bernstein inequality (Theorem 6.1.1) in Tropp et al. (2015). In particular, define S t = ( x t x /latticetop t -Σ x ) /τ for t ∈ [ τ ] . We have E ( S t ) = 0 and S t op ( x t x /latticetop op + Σ x op ) /τ 2 u 2 /τ for all t [ τ ] . Moreover, we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the first inequality follows from the fact that E ( S 1 S /latticetop 1 ) is positive semi-definite and Weyl's inequality. Thus, by Theorem 6.1.1 in Tropp et al. (2015), we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which completes the proof.

Lemma S.5.1 establishes a high-probability upper bound on the behavior of the sample design matrix of the GLM. Lemma S.5.1 serves as the basis for later theoretical results.

Lemma S.5.1. For τ ∈ N + , define V τ = ∑ τ t =1 x t x /latticetop t , where { x t } t ∈ [ τ ] are i.i.d. from an unknown distribution with ‖ x 1 ‖ ≤ u x , u x &gt; 0. Denote Σ x = E ( x 1 x /latticetop 1 ). There exist absolute constants c 1 , c 2 &gt; 0 such that for any δ ∈ (0 , 1 / 2), with probability at least 1 -δ , we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Proof of Lemma S.5.1 . Note that (i) can be directly derived based on the concentration inequality result in Proposition S.5.1 via simple algebra. As for (ii), first, it follows from (i) and Weyl's inequality that any δ ∈ (0 , 1 / 2), with probability at least 1 -δ , we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where ς = √ log(2 d ) + log(1 /δ ) / √ τ . Note that under (S.8), ς &lt; 1. We then have that, with probability at least 1 -δ ,

By simple algebra, (ii) is proved.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Proof of Theorem 2.1 . For notational convenience, in the proof, we omit the index τ when no confusion arises. Recalling that L ( θ ) = ∑ τ t =1 { y t x /latticetop t θ -ψ ( x /latticetop t θ ) } , let L ′ ( θ ) = ∑ τ t =1 { y t -ψ ′ ( x /latticetop t θ ) } x t

and L ′′ ( θ ) = -∑ τ t =1 ψ ′′ ( x /latticetop t θ ) x t x /latticetop t be the first and second order derivatives of L ( θ ). By the definitions of θ ∗ and ̂ θ , recalling that ε t = y t -ψ ′ ( x /latticetop t θ ∗ ), we have that L ′ ( θ ∗ ) = ∑ τ t =1 ε t x t and L ′ ( ̂ θ ) = 0. Define G ( θ ) = L ′ ( θ ∗ ) -L ′ ( θ ). We have that G ( θ ∗ ) = 0 and G ( ̂ θ ) = ∑ τ t =1 ε t x t .

## S.5.2 Technical details in Section S.3

Lemma S.5.2. Suppose Assumption 2.1 holds. For all z ∈ Z , the true revenue function r ( p, z, θ ∗ ) defined in (3) is L r -Lipschitz in p ∈ [ l, u ], with L r = M ψ 1 + uu b M ψ 2 . For all p ∈ [ l, u ] and z ∈ Z , it

holds that r ( p, z, θ ∗ ) ≤ uM ψ 1 and | ∂ 2 r ( p, z, θ ∗ ) /∂p 2 | ≤ C r = 2 M ψ 2 u b + uu 2 b M ψ 3 .

/negationslash

where the third equality follows from the mean value theorem with ˙ x /latticetop θ ∗ between x /latticetop θ ∗ and ˜ x /latticetop θ ∗ .

Therefore, we have that

where the second inequality follows from Assumption 2.1 and the definition of M ψ 1 and M ψ 2 . The other results can be proved similarly.

Lemma S.5.3 is adapted from Lemma 4 in Li et al. (2017) and Lemma 14 in Auer (2002) with a formal proof. Denote Ψ o s ( t ) = Ψ s ( t ) ∪ F s , where F s is the s th pure price experiment set and Ψ s ( t ) is the index set that collects all time periods in [ Sτ +1 , t -1] such that supCB stopped in stage s .

Lemma S.5.3. In Algorithm S.2, for all s ∈ [ S ] and t ∈ [ Sτ +1 , T ], given the features { x i , i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) } , the demands { y i , i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) } are conditionally independent GLM random variables with parameter θ ∗ .

Proof of Lemma S.5.3 . Note that we need to show

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

For notational simplicity, denote X s ≤ t = { x i : i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) } and Y s ≤ t = { y i : i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) } . For s ≥ 2, define Φ o &lt;s ( t ) = ⋃ σ&lt;s { Y σ ≤ t , X σ ≤ t } as the results observed at stages [ s -1] up to time t (not including t ). Furthermore, for time points t 1 &lt; t 2 , define Φ o &lt;s ( t 1 , t 2 ) = Φ o &lt;s ( t 2 ) \ Φ o &lt;s ( t 1 ) as the results observed at stages [ s -1] between time t 1 to t 2 -1, inclusive.

In the following, we show that for all t ∈ [ Sτ +1 , T ] and s ∈ [ S ], we have that

Note that by the property of conditional probability, (S.11) directly implies (S.10). We proceed by mathematical induction. First, note that for t = Sτ + 1, (S.11) clearly holds as Ψ o s ( Sτ + 1) = F s , which consists of only price experiments.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

/negationslash

Now, suppose (S.11) holds for t. Denote t + as the first time point such that Ψ o s ( t + ) \ Ψ o s ( t ) = ∅ . Note that clearly t + &gt; t and in fact we have Ψ o s ( t + ) \ Ψ o s ( t ) = t + -1. In other words, ( y t ∗ , x t ∗ ) is observed in stage s , where for notational simplicity, we define t ∗ = t + -1. To finish the proof, we only need to show that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

In particular, by the property of conditional probability, we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

We now analyze the above terms one by one.

- First, by (1), we have that P ( y t ∗ | Y s ≤ t , x t ∗ , X s ≤ t , Φ o &lt;s ( t ) , Φ o &lt;s ( t, t + )) = P ( y t ∗ | x t ∗ ).
- Second, importantly, by design, t ∗ can only be added to stage s in Step II of Algorithm S.2. For this to happen, it only depends on the results in the previous stages Φ o &lt;s ( t + ) and w ( s ) t ∗ ,a , and moreover, w ( s ) t ∗ ,a only depends on X s ≤ t but not Y s ≤ t . Therefore, the possible values of x t ∗ that leads to t ∗ being added in stage s is entirely determined by X s ≤ t and Φ o &lt;s ( t + ), and is not affected by the value of Y s ≤ t . In other words, we have the conditional independence result

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

- Third, by the design of Algorithm S.2, the observations Φ o &lt;s ( t, t + ) at stages [ s -1] does not depends on Y s ≤ t , X s ≤ t and therefore, we have P { Φ o &lt;s ( t, t + ) | Y s ≤ t , X s ≤ t , Φ o &lt;s ( t ) } = P { Φ o &lt;s ( t, t + ) | Φ o &lt;s ( t ) } .

Moreover, by the induction assumption, we have that P ( Y s ≤ t | X s ≤ t , Φ o &lt;s ( t )) = ∏ i ∈ Ψ o s ( t ) P ( y i | x i ). Therefore, we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which can be shown based on the exact same arguments as above and thus is omitted.

Theorem S.5.1 provides theoretical guarantees for the regret of supCB combined with the standard doubling trick described at the end of Section S.3. For a given dimension d , denote T ∗ d as the minimum T such that condition (S.2) holds with δ = 1 /T .

## S.5.3 Technical details in Section 2.2

Proposition S.5.2. Define R ς o = { ( α /latticetop z, β /latticetop z ) : z ∈ ¯ Z , ‖ ( α /latticetop , β /latticetop ) /latticetop -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ ς o } . Under Assumptions 2.1(a), (c), (e) and 2.2, there exists a bivariate Lipschitz continuous function ϕ ( · , · ) over R ς o with a Lipschitz constant C ϕ &gt; 0 such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for all z ∈ ¯ Z and θ ∈ { ( α /latticetop , β /latticetop ) : ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ ς o } ⊂ R 2 d . Moreover, C ϕ is an absolute constant that only depends on quantities in Assumptions 2.1 and 2.2.

## Proof of Proposition S.5.2 . By definition,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Thus, p ∗ depends on θ and z via α /latticetop z and β /latticetop z .

By Assumption 2.2, for all z ∈ ¯ Z and θ = ( α, β ) where ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ ς o , the optimal price p ∗ is unique. Therefore, for all ( α /latticetop z, β /latticetop z ) ∈ R ς o , we have that p ∗ is in fact a function of α /latticetop z and β /latticetop z and

can be written as p ∗ = ϕ ( α /latticetop z, β /latticetop z ). Note it is clear that R ς o is a compact set in R 2 and furthermore R ς o ⊆ { ( w,v ) : | w | ≤ u a + ς o , | v | ≤ u b + ς o } by Assumption 2.1(a) and (c).

We next prove that ϕ ( w,v ) is continuous over R ς o by Berge's Maximum Theorem (Berge, 1957). To apply Berge's Maximum Theorem, we first define Γ( w,v ) = [ l, u ] for any w,v . Note that Γ( w,v ) is a set-valued function and is continuous at any ( w,v ). By the definition of ϕ ( w,v ), we have that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

By Assumption 2.1(e), p · ψ ′ ( w -vp ) is a continuous function of ( p, w, v ). Together with the fact that Γ( w,v ) is continuous at any ( w,v ), by Berge's Maximum Theorem, we have that ϕ ( w,v ) (as a set-valued function) is upper hemicontinuous at any ( w,v ). Moreover, by the above discussion, we know that ϕ ( w,v ) is indeed a function over R ς o . Thus, by the definition of upper hemicontinuity, we have that ϕ ( w,v ) is continuous over R ς o .

We now further show that ϕ ( w,v ) is a continuously differentiable function over R ς o based on an application of the implicit function theorem. For any fixed ( w o , v o ) ∈ R ς o , by Assumption 2.2, there is a unique optimal price p ∗ ∈ ( l, u ) . Note that by Assumption 2.1(e), the revenue function r ( p ; w o , v o ) = p · ψ ′ ( w o -v o p ) is twice continuously differentiable. Define f ( p ; w o , v o ) = ψ ′ ( w o -v o p ) -pψ ′ ( w o -v o p ) v o , which is the derivative of r ( p ; w o , v o ) . Since p ∗ is the unique maximizer of r ( p ; w o , v o ), we therefore have that f ( p ∗ ; w o , v o ) = 0 and f ′ ( p ∗ ; w o , v o ) &lt; 0. Therefore, viewing f ( p ; w,v ) = ψ ′ ( w -vp ) -pψ ′ ( w -vp ) v as a trivariate function of ( w,v, p ), by the implicit function theorem, we have that there exists a continuously differentiable function g ( w,v ) (that may depend on ( w o , v o )) such that for all points ( w,v ) in a small neighborhood of ( u o , v o ), we have that f ( g ( w,v ) , w, v ) = 0 . By definition, this g ( w,v ) is the optimal price p ∗ at ( w,v ). On the other hand, by our above result, we know that p ∗ = ϕ ( w,v ). Therefore, we have that ϕ ( w,v ) = g ( w,v ) for all ( w,v ) in R ς o and thus ϕ ( w,v ) is continuously differentiable over R ς o , which completes the proof.

Proof of Theorem 2.3 . The proof is similar to that of Theorem S.5.1.

It is easy to see that the total rounds up to the k th episode is 2 k +1 -2 . For any T , there exists a k ∈ N + such that 2 k +1 -2 ≤ T &lt; 2 k +2 -2 . Denote T = 2 k +2 -2. Clearly, R T ≤ R T . <!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

To establish the high probability bound, we decompose the regret R T into R (1) , which accounts for regret from episode i = 1 , · · · , j -1, and R (2) , which accounts for regret from episode i = j, j + 1 , · · · , k + 1 . Denote E i = 2 i as the length of the i th episode. First, by Lemma S.5.2, we have that r ( p ∗ t , z t , θ ∗ ) ≤ uM ψ 1 , hence where the last inequality follows from the definition of j.

Second, by assumption, we know that E j ≥ √ dT ≥ T ∗ d . In other words, Theorem 2.2 applies to all episodes j, j + 1 , · · · , k + 1 . Note that ∑ k +1 i = j √ 2 i ≤ 2 . 5 √ 2 k +2 . For R (2) , by union bound and

Theorem 2.2, we have that with probability at least

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the second inequality holds by definition of j .

## S.5.4 The MLE-cycle algorithm and a simple modification

The MLE-Cycle algorithm is first proposed in Broder and Rusmevichientong (2012) for dynamic pricing under an unknown demand model with no covariates (i.e. context-free) and is shown to provide a regret of order O ( √ T ). We summarize MLE-Cycle in Algorithm S.4. A (slight) variant of MLE-Cycle is further introduced in Ban and Keskin (2021) for contextual dynamic pricing with a d -dimensional covariate and is shown to provide a regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T ).

The key idea of the MLE-Cycle algorithm is to divide the horizon N + into cycles of increasing lengths. In particular, the c th cycle is of length c + k , where k is a constant. Within each cycle, the first k rounds are allocated for price experiments and the next c rounds are used for price exploitation. By simple algebra, it is easy to see that for all sufficiently large T, the number of price experiments conducted till the T th round is of order O ( k √ T ).

In the above mentioned literature, k is set as an absolute constant (typically 2) and MLE-Cycle gives a regret of order O ( d √ T ), which is sub-optimal - supported by Theorem 4.1. The intuitive reason is that since k is a constant that does not increase with the dimension d , the number of exploration is of order O ( √ T ) instead of the desired O ( √ dT log( T )). In other words, the (original) MLE-Cycle algorithm under-explores. Therefore, a simple fix is to make k increase with the dimension d . In particular, in the modified MLE-Cycle algorithm, we set the length of price experiments for the c th

cycle as

By simple algebra, for all sufficiently large T, the number of price experiments conducted by the modified MLE-Cycle till the T th round is of order O ( √ dT log( T )). Using similar arguments as the ones in our proof of Theorem 2.2, we can show that, under similar conditions as the ones used in Theorem 2.3, the modified MLE-Cycle can achieve the near-optimal regret of order O ( √ dT log( T )).

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## Algorithm S.4 The MLE-Cycle algorithm for dynamic pricing

Input : Price interval [ l, u ], exploration length k and Ψ = ∅ .

for cycles c = 1 , 2 , · · · do c. (Estimation): Obtain MLE ̂ θ based on observations in Ψ. d. (Exploitation): For the next c rounds, offer the greedy price at p ∗ ( ̂ θ, z t ) based on ̂ θ . end for

a. (Exploration): For the first k rounds, randomly choose p t ∈ [ l, u ], and record y t and x t = ( z /latticetop t , -p t z /latticetop t ) /latticetop into the price experiment set Ψ.

## S.5.5 The Semi-Myopic algorithm and a simple modification

The Semi-Myopic type algorithm is a class of pricing policies modified from the greedy pricing policy and is proposed in Keskin and Zeevi (2014) for dynamic pricing under an unknown linear demand model with no covariates (i.e. context-free). Variants of Semi-Myopic algorithms have also been proposed in the literature, such as Controlled Variance in Den Boer and Zwart (2014) for context-free dynamic pricing and Random Shock Design in Simchi-Levi and Wang (2024) for contextual dynamic pricing (and its statistical inference problem).

These works show Semi-Myopic can provide a regret of order ˜ O ( √ T ), without investigating the impact of dimension d (i.e. treating the dimension d as fixed). In fact, one can easily show that for d -dimensional contextual dynamic pricing, these algorithms achieve a regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T ). We summarize a variant of Semi-Myopic in Algorithm S.5, which is based on the CILS algorithm in Keskin and Zeevi (2014) and the random shock design in Simchi-Levi and Wang (2024).

The key idea of the Semi-Myopic algorithm is to inject a carefully calibrated price deviation into the price given by the greedy algorithm (which is myopic), hence the name Semi-Myopic. In particular,

at each round t , given context z t , the firm offers a price at p ∗ ( ̂ θ, z t ) + δ t , where p ∗ ( ̂ θ, z t ) is the greedy price based on the estimated parameter ̂ θ and δ t = κt -1 / 4 · B t is the injected price deviation with B t being a Rademacher random variable. Importantly, one can show that thanks to the injected price deviation, for all sufficiently large t, the minimum eigenvalue of the design matrix based on observations in rounds { 1 , 2 , · · · , t } is guaranteed to be of order Ω( √ tλ z ), which is the key component for achieving a sublinear regret.

In the above mentioned literature, κ is set as an absolute constant (typically 1) and Semi-Myopic gives a regret of order ˜ O ( d √ T ), which is sub-optimal - supported by Theorem 4.1. The intuitive reason is that since κ is a constant that does not increase with the dimension d , making the minimum eigenvalue of the design matrix take the order Ω( √ tλ z ) instead of the desired Ω( √ dtλ z ). In other words, the (original) Semi-Myopic algorithm under-explores and injects less price deviation than the optimal level. Therefore, a simple fix is to make κ increase with the dimension d . In particular, in the modified Semi-Myopic algorithm, we instead set the price deviation for round t as κ = d 1 / 4 · t -1 / 4 . Using similar arguments as the ones in our proof of Theorem 2.2, we can show that, under similar conditions as the ones used in Theorem 2.3, the modified Semi-Myopic can achieve the near-optimal regret of O ( √ dT ).

˜ Computational consideration : Note that due to its design, Semi-Myopic require the computation of O ( T ) many MLEs, which is computationally much more intensive than MLE-Cycle and ETC. Therefore, in our implementation, we only run step (b) and (d) of Semi-Myopic every 5 rounds, which reduces its computational cost while still maintaining its theoretical guarantees.

## Algorithm S.5 The Semi-Myopic algorithm for dynamic pricing

Input : Price interval [ l, u ], exploration parameter κ and Ψ = ∅ .

for t = 1 , 2 , · · · do

- b. (Estimation): Obtain MLE ̂ θ based on observations. c. (Semi-Exploitation): Offer price p t at p ∗ ( ̂ θ, z t ) + δ t , where p ∗ ( ̂ θ, z t ) is the greedy price based on the MLE.

a. (Price deviation): Set δ t = κt -1 / 4 · B t , where B t is a Rademacher random variable.

d. (Record data): Record y t and x t = ( z /latticetop t , -p t z /latticetop t ) /latticetop into the set Ψ. end for

## S.6 Technical details in Section 3

## S.6.1 LDP guarantees

Lemma S.6.1 is directly from Section I.2 in Duchi et al. (2018) and we omit its proof. It ensures the LDP guarantees of Algorithm 4.

Lemma S.6.1. Let W( g ) be the output of L 2 -ball( g, C g , /epsilon1 ). Then, W( g ) is an /epsilon1 -LDP view of g , and E [W( g ) | g ] = g and ‖ W( g ) ‖ = C g r /epsilon1,d .

Proposition S.6.1. The LDP-ETC policy is /epsilon1 -LDP.

Proof of Proposition S.6.1 . Note that by design of ETC-LDP, for all θ ∈ Θ, ‖ g [ C ] t ( θ ; s t ) ‖ ≤ C g . Define the truncation mechanism of g t as the mapping of T : g t ↦→ g [ C ] t , and the L 2 -ball mechanism as W: g [ C ] t ↦→ w t , then our mechanism is a composition of truncation and L 2 -ball, i.e. W ◦ T : g t ↦→ w t . By definition, for all s t , s ′ t , w &lt;t , g t and g ′ t , we have for any measurable set S ,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the inequality holds by Lemma S.6.1 and the definition of LDP. This implies that W ◦ T is an /epsilon1 -LDP mechanism. We note that no privacy issue is involved for t &gt; τ .

The following lemma gives a uniform concentration inequality for martingale difference sequences that are bounded or satisfy certain moment conditions (e.g. sub-Gaussian), which is useful for bounding the variance component of the SGD estimator in Theorem 3.1 ( /epsilon1 -LDP), Lemma S.6.4 (mixed privacy constraint) and Lemma S.6.5 (approximate LDP). ## S.6.4 Proof of Theorem 3.3

Lemma S.6.4 gives a high-probability upper bound of the estimation error for the two-stage SGD estimator in the exploration phase of ETC-LDP-Mixed, serving as the basis for bounding the regret of ETC-LDP-Mixed. For convenience of presentation, we first recap the two-stage SGD estimator in ETC-LDP-Mixed with slightly different notations.

Suppose we have τ 1 private sample points under the /epsilon1 -LDP constraint and τ 2 non-private sample points. The two-stage SGD combines a private SGD and a non-private SGD to estimate the model parameter θ ∗ . In particular, it first runs a private SGD on the τ 1 private data as in Theorem 3.1, giving the output ̂ θ τ 1 . It then runs a non-private SGD with ̂ θ τ 1 as the initial point and step size

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

on the τ 2 non-private data, which gives the final estimator ̂ θ τ 1 + τ 2 .

We remark that for the non-private SGD, we use the true gradient instead of truncating the gradient to a bounded vector. In other words, the gradient can be potentially unbounded. Therefore, the technical argument for Lemma S.6.4 needs to be substantially modified compared to that in Theorem 3.1. In particular, we rely on the newly developed Lemma S.6.2 and Lemma S.6.3 to control the unbounded gradient. Technically, we extend the uniform concentration inequality for martingale difference sequences in Rakhlin et al. (2011) from bounded random variables to random variables with certain moment conditions (e.g. sub-Gaussian), which is of independent interest.

Lemma S.6.4. Suppose Assumption 2.1 and 3.1(a) hold. For any τ 1 , τ 2 ≥ 4, set C g = 2 √ 1 + u 2 [ M ∗ ψ 1 + σ √ log( τ 1 + τ 2 )] and ζ l = c l · φ z with some c l ∈ (0 , 1], where recall φ z = L p κ ∗ λ z . We have that, for any /epsilon1 &gt; 0 and 0 &lt; δ &lt; ( e ∨ log( τ 1 τ 2 )) -1 , with probability at least 1 -δ log( τ 1 τ 2 ), it holds that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where B M 1 &gt; 0 is an absolute constant that only depends on quantities in Assumptions 2.1 and 3.1(a).

Proof of Lemma S.6.4 . Note that the analysis of the first-stage private SGD on the τ 1 private

data follows exactly from Theorem 3.1. Therefore, we can directly analyze the performance of the non-private SGD on the τ 2 non-private data.

First, using the same argument as that in (S.26), we have
