<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

provided that T satisfies (S.39).

Taking the value of τ ( p ∗ ) = log T √ Td/ √ p ∗ +(1 -p ∗ ) /epsilon1 2 /d , and noting λ z /equivasymptotic ¯ λ z /equivasymptotic d -1 , we thus obtain that with high probability which completes the proof.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## S.6.5 Dynamic pricing under approximate LDP

Definition S.6.1 defines the notion of approximate LDP for dynamic pricing, which is modified from the notion of (pure) LDP for dynamic pricing in Definition 3.1 of the main text.

Definition S.6.1 (( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP for dynamic pricing) . For any /epsilon1 &gt; 0 and ∆ ∈ (0 , 1], a pricing policy π = { Q t , A t } T t =1 satisfies ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP if for any t ∈ [ T ], w &lt;t , and s t , s ′ t , it holds that for any measurable set W , Q t ( W | s t , w &lt;t ) ≤ e /epsilon1 Q t ( W | s ′ t , w &lt;t ) + ∆.

It is clear from the definition that ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP is a relaxed privacy constraint than /epsilon1 -LDP, as it allows a ∆ ∈ (0 , 1] probability of privacy leakage. In the following, we propose an ETC-LDPApprox algorithm in Algorithm S.6 and further establish its theoretical guarantees in Lemma S.6.5 and Theorem S.6.1. ETC-LDP-Approx in Algorithm S.6 follows the same structure as ETC-LDP in Algorithm 3. The only difference lies in their privacy mechanism. In particular, to achieve ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP, we employ the widely used Gaussian mechanism in ETC-LDP-Approx (see step a2) to privatize the truncated gradient g [ C ] t into w t , instead of the L 2 -ball mechanism in ETC-LDP.

Algorithm S.6 The ETC-LDP-Approx algorithm for dynamic pricing under ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP Input : Total rounds T , price interval [ l, u ], parameter space Θ , privacy parameter ( /epsilon1, ∆), exploration length τ , learning rate of SGD ζ l , gradient truncation parameter C g a. (Exploration) Randomly sample an initial estimator ̂ θ 0 from Θ. for t ∈ [ τ ] do a1. (Experiment): uniformly choose p t from [ l, u ], observe raw data s t = ( z t , y t , p t ) . a2. (Privatization): compute the truncated gradient g [ C ] t = g [ C ] t ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t ) = Π B Cg [ g ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t )] , with g ( ̂ θ t -1 ; s t ) defined in (11); let w t = g [ C ] t + ξ t , where ξ t ∼ N (0 , σ 2 ξ · I 2 d ) with σ 2 ξ = 2 C 2 g log(1 . 25 / ∆) //epsilon1 2 . a3. (SGD): update estimation via ̂ θ t = Π Θ [ ̂ θ t -1 + η t w t ] with step size η t = ( ζ l t ) -1 . end for b. (Exploitation) for t = τ +1 , · · · , T do offer the greedy price at p t = p ∗ ( ̂ θ τ , z t ) = arg max p ∈ [ l,u ] r ( p, z t , ̂ θ τ ) based on ̂ θ τ . end for

Lemma S.6.5 gives a high-probability upper bound of the estimation error for the ( /epsilon1, ∆)-private SGD estimator in the exploration phase of ETC-LDP-Approx, serving as the basis for bounding the

regret of ETC-LDP-Approx. In particular, it shows that the estimation error of θ ∗ under ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP is of the same order as that under /epsilon1 -LDP in Theorem 3.1 (up to a O (log(1 . 25 / ∆)) term).

Due to the Gaussian privacy mechanism, the gradient of the SGD is unbounded. To handle this, similar to the proof of Lemma S.6.4, we rely on the newly developed Lemma S.6.2 and Lemma S.6.3, which extends the uniform concentration inequality for martingale difference sequences in Rakhlin et al. (2011) from bounded random variables to random variables with certain moment conditions (e.g. subGaussian), and thus is of independent interest.

Lemma S.6.5. Under the conditions in Theorem 3.1 holds. For any τ ≥ 4, set the learning rate ζ l and truncation parameter C g same as in Theorem 3.1. We have that, for any /epsilon1 &gt; 0 , ∆ ∈ (0 , 1] and 0 &lt; δ &lt; ( e ∨ log τ ) -1 , with probability at least 1 -8 δ log τ , it holds that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where B A 1 &gt; 0 is an absolute constant that only depends on quantities in Assumptions 2.1 and 3.1(a).

Theorem S.6.1 gives the regret upper bound of the ETC-LDP-Approx algorithm in Algorithm S.6, which shows that the regret upper bound for dynamic pricing under ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP is of the same order as that under /epsilon1 -LDP in Theorem 3.2 (up to a O ( √ log(1 . 25 / ∆)) term). Its proof follows the same structure as that of Theorem 3.2 and is thus omitted.

Theorem S.6.1. Suppose conditions in Theorem 3.2 hold. For any /epsilon1 &gt; 0 and 0 &lt; δ &lt; { e ∨ log( T ) } -1 , set the learning rate ζ l and truncation parameter C g same as in Theorem 3.2. For any T satisfying T ≥ B A 2 · { λ -4 z /epsilon1 -2 log( T ) log(1 /δ ) } , we have that, with probability at least 1 -δ log( T ), the regret of ETCLDP-Approx is upper bounded by R T ≤ B A 3 d √ log( T ) log(1 /δ ) log(1 . 25 / ∆) T//epsilon1 , where B A 2 , B A 3 &gt; 0 are absolute constants only depending on quantities in Assumptions 2.1, 2.2 and 3.1.

## S.6.5.1 Numerical experiments

In this section, we further conduct numerical experiments to examine the finite sample performance of ETC-LDP-Approx under ( /epsilon1, ∆)-LDP and compare it with ETC-LDP.

In particular, we use the exact same simulation setting in Section 5.2.3 of the main text. For

ETC-LDP-Approx, we fix /epsilon1 = 1 and vary the privacy leakage probability ∆ ∈ { 10 -2 , 10 -3 } . The result under /epsilon1 ∈ { 2 , 4 } is similar and thus omitted to conserve space.

Figure S.12(left) reports the mean regret R T,d,/epsilon1 of ETC-LDP-Approx with ∆ ∈ { 10 -2 , 10 -3 } at different ( T, d ) and /epsilon1 = 1 under (S1). As can be seen, given a fixed d , the regret scales sublinearly with respect to T , and given a fixed horizon T , the regret grows with the dimension d. Moreover, a smaller privacy leakage probability ∆ leads to a higher regret, which is intuitive and confirms the theoretical results in Theorem S.6.1.

For illustration, Figure S.12(middle) further gives the boxplot of { R ( i ) T,d,/epsilon1 } 500 i =1 achieved by ETCLDP (i.e. pure /epsilon1 -LDP) and ETC-LDP-Approx (at ∆ = 10 -2 ) for each T ∈ { (1 , 3 , 5 , 7 , 9) × 10 5 } and /epsilon1 = 1 with a fixed d = 6 . As can be seen, the regret given by the two algorithms are similar with ETC-LDP giving slightly lower regret (and smaller variance). This confirms our theoretical results that the two algorithms share the same order of regret upper bound up to a O ( √ log(1 . 25 / ∆) term. Figure S.12(right) gives the boxplot of { R ( i ) T,d,/epsilon1 } 500 i =1 achieved by ETC-LDP (i.e. pure /epsilon1 -LDP) and ETCLDP-Approx (at ∆ = 10 -2 ) for each d ∈ { 1 , 2 , 4 , 6 } and /epsilon1 = 1 with a fixed T = 9 × 10 5 , where similar phenomenon is observed. In addition, Figure S.13 gives the performance of ETC-LDP-Approx under (S2), where similar phenomenon as the one in Figure S.12 is observed.

Figure S.12: Performance of ETC-LDP-Approx under (S1). [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ) and /epsilon1 = 1 for ∆ = (10 -2 , 10 -3 ). [Middle]: Boxplot of regrets at different T (with d = 6 and /epsilon1 = 1) for pure-LDP and approx-LDP (∆ = 10 -2 ). [Right]: Boxplot of regrets at different d (with T = 9 × 10 5 and /epsilon1 = 1) for pure-LDP and approx-LDP (∆ = 10 -2 ).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure S.13: Performance of ETC-LDP-Approx under (S2). [Left]: Mean regret (with C.I.) under different ( d, T ) and /epsilon1 = 1 for ∆ = (10 -2 , 10 -3 ). [Middle]: Boxplot of regrets at different T (with d = 6 and /epsilon1 = 1) for pure-LDP and approx-LDP (∆ = 10 -2 ). [Right]: Boxplot of regrets at different d (with T = 9 × 10 5 and /epsilon1 = 1) for pure-LDP and approx-LDP (∆ = 10 -2 ).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## S.7 Technical details in Section 4

## S.7.1 Roadmap of the proof

In this subsection, we first provide a roadmap of the technical proof for the lower bound construction under both non-private and private dynamic pricing, where we point out key steps and key lemmas used in the proof and discuss their intuition. There are in total 4 steps (Step 0 - Step 3).

## Step 0. Lower bound construction. [Section S.7.2]

We first construct a set of 2 d revenue functions, indexed by the hyper parameter v = ( v 1 , · · · , v d ) /latticetop ∈ { 0 , 1 } d such that distinguishing v i = 0 from v i = 1 for each coordinate can only depend on the information from the i th covariate. Specifically, let z t = ( I ( M t = 1) , · · · , I ( M t = d )) /latticetop ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , where M t is a multinomial distribution on { 1 , · · · , d } with equal marginal probability. Consider the following demand model indexed by the hyper parameter v = ( v 1 , · · · , v d ) /latticetop ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , such that where { ε t } T t =1 is a sequence of independent N (0 , 1) random variables, and ∆ ∈ [0 , 1 / 2] quantifies the difficulty of the problem, and will be chosen differently for the non-private and private cases. Note by our construction, the effective information for v i is contained through the i th element of z t , i.e. I ( M t = i ) only.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

For M t = i , an important property of our construction is the following connection of the instant

regret and the policy induced price:

Step 1. Reduction to classification problem. [Lemma S.7.1 for the non-private case, and Lemma S.7.4 for the private case.]

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

[ The non-private case, Lemma S.7.1 ] We first construct a classifier using data s t = { z t , p t , y t } such that ψ : ( s 1 , · · · , s T ) → { ˆ v i } d i =1 ∈ { 0 , 1 } d . In Lemma S.7.1, we show, with high probability, for any non-anticipating policy π ,

/negationslash

This implies that if there is a low-regret pricing policy, then there is a classifier ψ that achieves small classification errors. Therefore, this motivates us to use information-theoretical tools (Assouad's Lemma in particular) to show a lower bound on the classification error.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

[ The private case, Lemma S.7.4 ] Under the LDP constraint, we cannot directly use the classifier defined in the non-private setting as it utilizes non-privatized contextual information. Instead, we adopt the construction in Lemma 3 of Chen et al. (2022a) and augment the /epsilon1 -LDP policy π to construct a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP policy π ′ . In particular, we have to additionally privatize the information used for classification, which necessitates elevating the regret analysis from an /epsilon1 -LDP policy to a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP policy. To address this, we privatize the information used in the classification process by introducing Laplacian noise into the response vector of the classification. Lemma S.7.4 then shows that, with high probability, for any non-anticipating /epsilon1 -LDP policy π , there exists a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP classifier ψ : ( w 1 , · · · , w T ) → ˆ v ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , such that (S.50) still holds.

Step 2. Assouad's Lemma to lower bound the classification error. [Lemma S.7.2 for the non-private case, and Lemma S.7.8 for the private case.]

[ The non-private case, Lemma S.7.2 ] To introduce the method, we define some additional nota-

tions. For the non-private case, define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

with P π v being the joint distribution of { s 1 , · · · , s T } under model v and policy π . Then, we have

/negationslash where the first inequality holds by Assouad's Lemma, the second by Pinsker's inequality, and the last by the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.

Therefore, it suffices to calculate the KL divergence between P π + i and P π -i . Thanks to the Gaussian design of the demand model, and the chain-rule of KL divergence using the conditioning argument, we manage to show that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

This further connects the classification error with the regret via (S.49).

[ The private case, Lemma S.7.8 ] This is the key component of our lower bound construction to derive the Ω( d √ T//epsilon1 ) sharp bound under LDP. First, same as the non-private case, we begin by applying the standard Assouad's Lemma to connect the classification error with TV. Next, however, instead of using the KL divergence as in the non-private case, we resort to the sharper Hellinger distance to further bound TV. In fact, for two arbitrary distributions P 1 and P 2 , we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where H 2 ( P 1 , P 2 ) = 1 2 ∫ X [ √ P 1 (d x ) -P 2 (d x )] 2 is the Hellinger distance between P 1 and P 2 on the support X . The above inequality implies that using the Hellinger distance allows for more delicate analysis than the KL divergence.

Specifically, in the proof of Lemma S.7.8, we first show that,

/negationslash here M π + and M π -can be viewed as the private version of P π + and P π -respectively. We then ap-

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

ply a chain-rule type inequality (Lemma 4 in Jayram (2009)) to the Hellinger distance of the joint distribution above, decomposing it into the instant Hellinger distance associated with each time point.

We then adapt the newly-developed information contraction bound technique in Acharya et al. (2024), which is designed for private statistical estimation problem under i.i.d. data, to further bound the sum of Hellinger distance under our setting, where the data can be adaptively generated by any non-anticipating pricing policy with /epsilon1 -LDP. In particular, we manage to show that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which is our key result. Note that, compared with the non-private version, the bound above inflate by a factor of [exp(2 /epsilon1 ) -1] 2 /d /equivasymptotic /epsilon1 2 /d , which is the price to pay for protecting privacy.

The proof of Lemma S.7.8 is a key component and novelty of our lower bound construction and we give a more detailed discussion of this step in Step 2A below, where we discuss the technical challenge and important modifications we made to adapt the information contraction bound in Acharya et al. (2024) (designed for i.i.d. data) to the setting of private dynamic pricing (where data is adaptive).

We remark that our analysis in Lemma S.7.8 is new and first time seen in the dynamic pricing literature. In fact, existing lower bound construction in contextual bandit (Han et al., 2021) and contextual (non-parametric) dynamic pricing (Chen et al., 2022a) under LDP constraints mainly rely on the well-known private Le Cam Lemma (Theorem 1 therein) and private Assouad's Lemma (Proposition 3 therein) developed in Duchi et al. (2018), combined with KL divergence bounds. However, we find that this strategy (i.e. technical tools in Duchi et al. (2018) + KL divergence) can only derive a Ω( √ dT//epsilon1 ) lower bound, which is not sharp in dimension d. Instead, our proof strategy is to leverage the Hellinger distance and modify the information contraction bound in Acharya et al. (2024) to derive the sharp Ω( d √ T//epsilon1 ) bound.

Step 3. Finish the proof . Therefore, to summarize, for both the non-private and private cases, we effectively create a dilemma for the regret of any non-anticipating policy. In particular, for small values of ∆, we can always identify a setting where the associated classification error is large due to the Assouad's Lemma. Conversely, for large values of ∆, as is implied by (S.49), the cumulative

regret also becomes large. The result then follows by carefully balancing two scenarios and choosing an appropriate value of ∆.

Step 2A. Key modification of Acharya et al. (2024) for LDP dynamic pricing [Step II in the proof Lemma S.7.8 and Lemma S.7.9.]

We first introduce the information contraction bound result in Acharya et al. (2024), which applies to statistical estimation problems under /epsilon1 -LDP but for i.i.d. data only. We then discuss the key nontrivial modifications we made to tailor for dynamic pricing under /epsilon1 -LDP, where the data is no longer i.i.d. but instead can be adaptively collected by any non-anticipating pricing policy π.

Results in Acharya et al. (2024) . Let { P v : v ∈ { 0 , 1 } d } be a family of distributions indexed by v . Let P T + i = 1 2 d -1 ∑ v i =1 P v , P T -i = 1 2 d -1 ∑ v i =0 P T v , where P T v denotes the joint distribution of T i.i.d. samples { x t ∈ X} T t =1 whose marginal distribution is P v . Furthermore, define the sequentially interactive /epsilon1 -LDP mechanism Q that output privatized data w t given input x t and past information w &lt;t , i.e.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where M t, v ( · ) denotes the marginal distribution of w t , and M v ( w 1 , · · · , w t ) is the joint distribution of { w s } t s =1 indexed by v . Define M T + i = 1 2 d -1 ∑ v i =1 M v ( w 1: T ), M T -i = 1 2 d -1 ∑ v i =0 M v ( w 1: T ).

/negationslash which flips the i th coordinate of v from v i to 1 -v i . Then, the following two assumptions are imposed.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Acharya-Assumption 1 (Densities Exist). For every v ∈ { 0 , 1 } d and i ∈ [ d ], it holds that p v ⊕ i /lessmuch p v , and there exist measurable functions ϕ v ,i : X → R such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The functions ϕ v ,i are referred to as 'score function' and capture the change in density when the coordinate i is flipped.

Acharya-Assumption 2 (Orthogonality and Variance). There exists some α 2 ≥ 0 such

Further define v ⊕ i as the vector with coordinates,

that, for all v ∈ { 0 , 1 } d and distinct i, j ∈ [ d ],

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The following result is taken from Corollary 1 in Acharya et al. (2024).

[Corollary 1 in Acharya et al. (2024)] Suppose the family of distributions { P v : v ∈ { 0 , 1 } d } satisfies the Acharya-Assumptions 1 and 2 above. Then, for any sequentially /epsilon1 -LDP mechanism, we have

The proof of Corollary 1 uses similar idea in our Step 2 above by bounding the total variation distance with the Hellinger distance as an intermediate step. The multiplicative factor [exp( /epsilon1 ) -1] 2 /d /equivasymptotic /epsilon1 2 /d characterizes the cost of privacy.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Key modifications made to tailor for dynamic pricing under /epsilon1 -LDP . The result Acharya et al. (2024) only applies to i.i.d. data and thus is not directly applicable to dynamic pricing under /epsilon1 -LDP, where the data { s t = ( y t , z t , p t ) } can be adaptively generated by any non-anticipating pricing policy π . In particular, note that the price p t given by π at any time point t is a function of the current covariate z t and all historical private observations w &lt;t = { w 1 , · · · , w t -1 } (see (9) and (10) in the main text), which clearly makes { s t } non-i.i.d. with complex inter-dependence. Therefore, non-trivial modification is required to adapt the technical tools in Acharya et al. (2024) to our setting of dynamic pricing under /epsilon1 -LDP. Our main technical contributions are as follows.

First, to address the complex inter-dependence of the raw data { s t } , we develop a novel decomposition argument and show that instead of analyzing the (intractable) marginal distribution of s t due to adaptivity of π , we can analyze the conditional distribution of s t given past private observations w &lt;t , denoted by dP π v ,t ( s t | w &lt;t ) /ds t . Importantly, leveraging the two-part structure of the LDP pricing policy π , as described in (9) and (10) of the main text, we show that dP π v ,t ( s t | w &lt;t ) /ds t is in fact tractable. Denote ϕ v ,i ( s t | w &lt;t ) as the score function defined as in Acharya-Assumption 1 above but for dP π v ,t ( s t | w &lt;t ) /ds t , we show in Lemma S.7.9 that ϕ v ,i ( s t | w &lt;t ) still exhibits nice structures. In particular, ϕ v ,i ( s t | w &lt;t ) is a martingale difference and the orthogonality condition in Acharya-Assumption 2

above still holds for ϕ v ,i ( s t | w &lt;t ). This is indeed intuitive as by our lower bound construction in Step 0, the instant information gain at time t stems solely from the current active dimension i , with no contribution from other inactive dimensions.

Second, Acharya-Assumption 2 imposes a constant bound α on the variation of the score function ϕ v ,i , which is sufficient for lower bound construction for statistical estimation. However, this is not sufficient for our purposes as we need to establish a bound on the TV that is a function of the regret of π as is in (S.51). We address this by deriving a new upper bound on the variance of ϕ v ,i . In particular, in the proof of Lemma S.7.9, we show its variance can be further upper bounded by a quadratic term in both the policy-induced price and the optimal price, which subsequently leads to the derivation of the instant regret due to (S.49).

## S.7.2 Lower bound construction

At a high level, we construct a set of 2 d revenue functions, indexed by the hyper parameter v = ( v 1 , · · · , v d ) /latticetop ∈ { 0 , 1 } d such that distinguishing v i = 0 from v i = 1 for each coordinate can only depend on the information from the i th covariate. This implies that learning the parameters associated with the i th covariate can only make use of the data sample of effective sample size of order O ( T/d ). In addition, by carefully crafting the model parameters, the revenue functions in the set are difficult to distinguish. Therefore, it is very costly to differentiate these functions, which will inevitably incur large regret.

Lower bound construction Let z t = ( I ( M t = 1) , . . . , I ( M t = d )) /latticetop ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , where M t is a multinomial distribution on { 1 , . . . , d } with equal marginal probability. Consider the following demand model indexed by the hyper parameter v = ( v 1 , . . . , v d ) /latticetop ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , such that where { ε t } T t =1 is a sequence of independent N (0 , 1) random variables, and ∆ ∈ [0 , 1 / 2] is a small quantity to be defined later.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Therefore, the instant revenue function (conditional on z t , p t ) is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

This further implies that conditional on { M t = i } where i ∈ { 1 , . . . , d } , we have that the optimal price takes the form

For M t = i , the instant regret associated with the policy π is given by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Define S p = { p t : | p t -1 | ≤ ∆ / 6 } . For ∆ ≤ 1 / 2, it is clear that combined with (S.54), we have for M t = i , it holds that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The property in (S.55) plays an important role in the subsequent analysis.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Proposition S.7.1 states that our lower bound instances constructed later in the proof of Theorems 4.1 and 4.2 satisfy the corresponding assumptions in the upper bound results.

Proposition S.7.1. The lower bound construction in Theorems 4.1 and 4.2 satisfies Assumption 2.1 and 2.2. If T ≥ c/epsilon1 -2 d 4 for some constant c &gt; 0, then the parameter space in Theorem 4.2 satisfies Assumption 3.1.

Proof. For Assumption 2.1: Clearly, we have ‖ z t ‖ = 1 and Σ z = d -1 I d , hence (a) and (b) are satisfied. Since α ∗ = 2 + ∆ v , β ∗ = 1 + ∆ v , and ‖ v ‖ ∞ ≤ 1 , we have | z /latticetop t β ∗ | ≤ | z /latticetop t α ∗ | ≤ 2 + ∆ ≤ 3 . This implies that (c) is satisfied. The verification for (d) and (e) is trivial for linear model with Gaussian error.

For Assumption 2.2: We next show that it holds for ς o = 1 / 16 and [ l, u ] = [1 / 3 , 3 / 2] . Due to the linearity of the demand function, we have that for any θ , r ( p, z t , θ ) is uniquely maximized at p ∗ t ( z t , θ ) = z /latticetop t α/z /latticetop t β . It suffices to show that for ‖ θ -θ ∗ ‖ ≤ ς o , we have p ∗ t ( z t , θ ) ∈ [1 / 3 , 3 / 2]. Without loss of generality, we can assume M t = i , and hence p ∗ t ( z t , θ ) = α i /β i . We thus have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the last inequality holds by | α i -α ∗ i | , | β i -β ∗ i | ≤ ς o . Using α ∗ i ≤ 3 , and β ∗ i ≥ 1, we have

| p ∗ t ( z t , θ ) -p ∗ t ( z t , θ ∗ ) | ≤ ς o / (1 -ς o ) + 3 ς o / (1 -ς o ) 2 = (4 ς o -3 ς 2 o ) / (1 -ς o ) 2 ≤ 61 / 225 &lt; 1 / 2 . Clearly, in view of (S.53), for ∆ ≤ 1 / 2, we have p ∗ t ( z t , θ ∗ ) ∈ [5 / 6 , 1] ⊂ [1 / 3 , 3 / 2]. This further implies that p ∗ t ( z t , θ ) ∈ [1 / 3 , 3 / 2].

For Assumption 3.1: Note that in the proof of Theorem 4.2, we let ∆ 2 = √ 14 d/ [672 /epsilon1 √ T ]. Hence, for any pairs of v 1 , v 2 ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , if T ≥ c/epsilon1 -2 d 4 , we have ‖ θ v 1 -θ v 2 ‖ 2 ≤ 2 d ∆ 2 = √ 14 / 336 /epsilon1 -1 d 2 T -1 / 2 ≤ √ 14 /c/ 336. Hence, letting c 2 θ = √ 14 /c/ 336 is sufficient for (a). Part (b) is trivial.

## S.7.4 Proof of Theorem 4.2

The following lemma is a privatized version of Lemma S.7.1, which lower bounds the regret by a classification error. In particular, it shows that the regret of any /epsilon1 -LDP policy π can be lower bounded by the classification error of an associated 2 /epsilon1 -LDP classifier.

Lemma S.7.4. Suppose √ T ≥ 34 d/epsilon1 -1 √ log( T ). For any non-anticipating /epsilon1 -LDP policy π , there exists a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP classifier ψ : ( w 1 , · · · , w T ) → ˆ v ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , such that with probability at least 1 -5 dT -2 , we

/negationslash

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

/negationslash

/negationslash

have

/negationslash

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Proof. For clarity, we structure the proof into three components.

[A High probability event A /epsilon1 ] : We construct i.i.d. Laplace random variables { η ti } T,d t =1 ,i =1 such that η ti ∼ Lap(2 //epsilon1 ). Let { η ′ ti } T,d t =1 ,i =1 be an independent copy of { η ti } T,d t =1 ,i =1 .

For each i ∈ [ d ], by a concentration inequality for Laplace random variables (e.g. Corollary 2.9 in Chan et al., 2011), for √ T &gt; 2log( T ), we have

Furthermore, by Hoeffding's inequality in (S.56), we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

We define A /epsilon1 i as the intersection of the above high probability events, i.e.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

[Design a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP classifier] : Note that we cannot directly use the classifier defined in the non-private setting as it utilizes non-privatized personal information in (S.57). Instead, we adopt the construction in Lemma 3 of Chen et al. (2022a) and augment the /epsilon1 -LDP policy π to construct a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP policy π ′ . In particular, we have to additionally privatize the information used for classification, which necessitates elevating the regret analysis from an /epsilon1 -LDP policy to a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP policy. The resulting lower bound is thus inflated by a multiplicative constant.

Therefore, we have that P ( A /epsilon1 i ) ≥ 1 -5 T -2 . The following analysis is conducted on the event A /epsilon1 = ⋂ d i =1 A /epsilon1 i , which, by a union bound argument, holds with probability larger than 1 -5 dT -2 .

Recall that w t is the intermediate quantities produced by policy π , such that the distribution of w t is measurable conditional on s t and w 1 , · · · , w t -1 . We construct augmented intermediate quantity w ′ t = ( w t , { γ ti } d i =1 , { ζ ti } d i =1 ) such that for i ∈ { 1 , · · · , d } ,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Note that w ′ t satisfies 2 /epsilon1 -LDP thanks to the Laplace mechanism and simple composition of two /epsilon1 -LDP procedures, see, e.g. Dwork et al. (2014). This indicates that π ′ is a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP policy. Furthermore, note that by construction, the policy π ′ has the exact same regret as π .

Define ˆ γ i = ∑ T t =1 γ ti and ˆ ζ i = ∑ T t =1 ζ ti . It is clear that, ˆ γ i -γ i = ∑ T t =1 η ti and ˆ ζ i -ζ i = ∑ T t =1 η ′ ti , where recall γ i , ζ i are defined in (S.57). Therefore, under A /epsilon1 i , we have

We then construct the classifier ψ : ( w ′ 1 , · · · , w ′ T ) →{ ˆ v i } d i =1 ∈ { 0 , 1 } d such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Note that by construction, ψ is a 2 /epsilon1 -LDP classifier.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

[Regret lower bound by classification error] : We now analyze the consequence of misclassification. Suppose v i = 0 and ˆ v i = 1. This means ˆ γ i ≤ ˆ ζ i , and hence

This and (S.65) further imply that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Recall on A /epsilon1 i , we have ∑ T t =1 I { M t = i } ≥ T/d -√ T log( T ). Hence, for /epsilon1 ∈ (0 , 1) , and √ T ≥ 34 d/epsilon1 -1 √ log( T ), by (S.66), we have

Similarly, if v i = 1 and ˆ v i = 0, we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Hence, if ˆ v i = v i , we have either γ i or ζ i is at least T/ (4 d ).

/negationslash

By similar arguments as the proof of Lemma S.7.1 (after equation (S.59)), we have that

/negationslash

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Similar to Lemma S.7.2, we need to lower bound the classification error under LDP by the total variation distance between distributions due to Assouad's Lemma. However, it turns out that the proof technique in Lemma S.7.2 using KL divergence to bound total variation distance will yield a less sharp result in terms of the dimension d . Instead, we establish a sharp bound using the Hellinger distance. In fact, for two arbitrary distributions P 1 and P 2 , we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where H 2 ( P 1 , P 2 ) = 1 2 ∫ X [ √ P 1 (d x ) -P 2 (d x )] 2 is the Hellinger distance between P 1 and P 2 on the support X . The above inequality implies that using the Hellinger distance allows for more delicate analysis than the KL divergence.

We first list necessary notation. Define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

with M π v being the distribution of { w 1 , · · · , w T } under model v and policy π (in fact, the 2 /epsilon1 -LDP policy π ′ to be precise). More specifically, we have

where S is the support of s t , and P π v ,t ( s t | w &lt;t ) is the joint distribution of s t given past privatized information w &lt;t under policy π and model parameter v . Let the density function of M π v ( ·| w &lt;t ) be

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

For any v = ( v 1 , · · · , v d ) /latticetop ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , we define v ⊕ i = ( v 1 , · · · , v i -1 , 1 -v i , v i +1 , · · · , v d ) /latticetop by changing the i th element in v . For a set A ⊂ [ T ], we let as the joint distribution function by substituting the t th conditional distribution of M π v ( w t | w &lt;t ) by M π v ⊕ i ( w t | w &lt;t ) for t ∈ A. Define its density function as m π v ⊕ i ,A , and write M π v ⊕ i , { t } = M π v ⊕ i ,t . It is also clear that M π v ⊕ i , ∅ = M π v .

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Now, we present some fundamental properties of the Hellinger distance.

## References

- Abbasi-Yadkori, Y., P´ al, D., and Szepesv´ ari, C. (2011). Improved algorithms for linear stochastic bandits. Advances in neural information processing systems , 24.
- Acharya, J., Canonne, C. L., Sun, Z., and Tyagi, H. (2024). Unified lower bounds for interactive highdimensional estimation under information constraints. Neural Information Processing Systems , 36.
- Auer, P. (2002). Using confidence bounds for exploitation-exploration trade-offs. Journal of Machine Learning Research , 3(Nov):397-422.
- Auer, P., Cesa-Bianchi, N., Freund, Y., and Schapire, R. E. (1995). Gambling in a rigged casino: The adversarial multi-armed bandit problem. In IEEE FOCS , pages 322-331.
- Ban, G.-Y. and Keskin, N. B. (2021). Personalized dynamic pricing with machine learning: Highdimensional features and heterogeneous elasticity. Management Science , 67(9):5549-5568.
- Berge, C. (1957). Two theorems in graph theory. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences , 43(9):842-844.
- Besbes, O. and Zeevi, A. (2009). Dynamic pricing without knowing the demand function: Risk bounds and near-optimal algorithms. Operations Research , 57(6):1407-1420.
- Broder, J. and Rusmevichientong, P. (2012). Dynamic pricing under a general parametric choice model. Operations Research , 60(4):965-980.
- Chan, T.-H. H., Shi, E., and Song, D. (2011). Private and continual release of statistics. ACM Transactions on Information and System Security (TISSEC) , 14(3):1-24.
- Chen, K., Hu, I., and Ying, Z. (1999). Strong consistency of maximum quasi-likelihood estimators in generalized linear models with fixed and adaptive designs. The Annals of Statistics , 27(4):1155-1163.
- Chen, N. and Gallego, G. (2021). Nonparametric pricing analytics with customer covariates. Operations Research , 69(3):974-984.

- Chen, X., Miao, S., and Wang, Y. (2022a). Differential privacy in personalized pricing with nonparametric demand models. Operations Research .
- Chen, X., Simchi-Levi, D., and Wang, Y. (2022b). Privacy-preserving dynamic personalized pricing with demand learning. Management Science , 68(7):4878-4898.
- Chen, X., Simchi-Levi, D., and Wang, Y. (2023). Utility fairness in contextual dynamic pricing with demand learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2311.16528 .
- Chu, W., Li, L., Reyzin, L., and Schapire, R. (2011). Contextual bandits with linear payoff functions. In Proceedings of the Fourteenth International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics , pages 208-214. JMLR Workshop and Conference Proceedings.
- Dani, V., Hayes, T. P., and Kakade, S. M. (2008). Stochastic linear optimization under bandit feedback. In COLT , volume 2, page 3.
- de la Pena, V. H. (1999). A general class of exponential inequalities for martingales and ratios. The Annals of Probability , 27(1):537-564.
- Den Boer, A. V. and Zwart, B. (2014). Simultaneously learning and optimizing using controlled variance pricing. Management science , 60(3):770-783.
- Duchi, J. C., Jordan, M. I., and Wainwright, M. J. (2018). Minimax optimal procedures for locally private estimation. Journal of the American Statistical Association , 113(521):182-201.
- Dwork, C., McSherry, F., Nissim, K., and Smith, A. (2006). Calibrating noise to sensitivity in private data analysis. In TCC 2006 , pages 265-284.
- Dwork, C., Roth, A., et al. (2014). The algorithmic foundations of differential privacy. Foundations and Trends ® in Theoretical Computer Science , 9(3-4):211-407.
- Evfimievski, A., Gehrke, J., and Srikant, R. (2003). Limiting privacy breaches in privacy preserving data mining. In ACM SIGMOD-SIGACT-SIGART , pages 211-222.

- Fan, J., Guo, Y., and Yu, M. (2024). Policy optimization using semiparametric models for dynamic pricing. Journal of the American Statistical Association , 119(545):552-564.
- Filippi, S., Cappe, O., Garivier, A., and Szepesv´ ari, C. (2010). Parametric bandits: The generalized linear case. Advances in neural information processing systems , 23.
- Han, Y., Liang, Z., Wang, Y., and Zhang, J. (2021). Generalized linear bandits with local differential privacy. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems , 34:26511-26522.
- Hsu, D., Kakade, S. M., and Zhang, T. (2012). A tail inequality for quadratic forms of subgaussian random vectors. Electronic Communications in Probability , 17:1.
- Javanmard, A. and Nazerzadeh, H. (2019). Dynamic pricing in high-dimensions. Journal of Machine Learning Research , 20(1):315-363.
- Jayram, T. S. (2009). Hellinger strikes back: A note on the multi-party information complexity of and. In Dinur, I., Jansen, K., Naor, J., and Rolim, J., editors, Approximation, Randomization, and Combinatorial Optimization. Algorithms and Techniques , pages 562-573, Berlin, Heidelberg. Springer Berlin Heidelberg.
- Keskin, N. B. and Zeevi, A. (2014). Dynamic pricing with an unknown demand model: Asymptotically optimal semi-myopic policies. Operations Research , 62(5):1142-1167.
- Lai, T. L. and Robbins, H. (1985). Asymptotically efficient adaptive allocation rules. Advances in applied mathematics , 6(1):4-22.
- Lei, Y., Miao, S., and Momot, R. (2023). Privacy-preserving personalized revenue management. Management Science .
- Li, L., Lu, Y., and Zhou, D. (2017). Provably optimal algorithms for generalized linear contextual bandits. In International Conference on Machine Learning , pages 2071-2080. PMLR.
- Liu, P., Yang, Z., Wang, Z., and Sun, W. W. (2023). Contextual dynamic pricing with strategic buyers. arXiv preprint arXiv:2307.04055 .

- Luo, Y., Sun, W. W., and Liu, Y. (2024). Distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing. Mathematics of Operations Research , 49(1):599-618.
- Qiang, S. and Bayati, M. (2016). Dynamic pricing with demand covariates. arXiv preprint arXiv:1604.07463 .
- Rakhlin, A., Shamir, O., and Sridharan, K. (2011). Making gradient descent optimal for strongly convex stochastic optimization. arXiv preprint arXiv:1109.5647 .
- Rigollet, P. and H¨ utter, J.-C. (2023). High-dimensional statistics. arXiv preprint arXiv:2310.19244 .
